[1st century AD](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century")  [Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)"), Roman Empire

est. [2.3 billion](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country")[[7]](#cite_note-8) Increase ([referred to](/wiki/List_of_Christian_synonyms "List of Christian synonyms") as [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"))

infobox.key: [Principal symbol of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_cross "Principal symbol of Christianity")
infobox.value: | * [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") * [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")  * [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") * [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") * [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") * [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") * [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") * [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")

is an [Abrahamic](/wiki/Abrahamic "Abrahamic") [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheistic "Monotheistic") [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), which states that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") is the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(Christianity) "Son of God (Christianity)") and [rose from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") after [his crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), whose coming as the [messiah](/wiki/Messiah#Christianity "Messiah") ([Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")) was [prophesied](/wiki/Old_Testament_messianic_prophecies_quoted_in_the_New_Testament "Old Testament messianic prophecies quoted in the New Testament") in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and chronicled in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"). It is the [world's largest](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups") and most widespread religion with over 2.3 billion followers, comprising around 28.8% of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population"). Its adherents, known as [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), are estimated to make up a majority of the population in [120 countries and territories](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country").

**Christianity** is an [Abrahamic](/wiki/Abrahamic "Abrahamic") [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheistic "Monotheistic") [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), which states that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") is the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(Christianity) "Son of God (Christianity)") and [rose from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") after [his crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), whose coming as the [messiah](/wiki/Messiah#Christianity "Messiah") ([Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")) was [prophesied](/wiki/Old_Testament_messianic_prophecies_quoted_in_the_New_Testament "Old Testament messianic prophecies quoted in the New Testament") in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") and chronicled in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"). It is the [world's largest](/wiki/Major_religious_groups "Major religious groups") and most widespread religion with over 2.3 billion followers, comprising around 28.8% of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population"). Its adherents, known as [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians"), are estimated to make up a majority of the population in [120 countries and territories](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country").

Christianity remains [culturally diverse](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture") in its [Western](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") and [Eastern branches](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), and doctrinally diverse concerning [justification and the nature of salvation](/wiki/Justification_(theology) "Justification (theology)"), [ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology"), [ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination"), and [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology"). Most [Christian denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations"), however, generally hold in common the belief that Jesus is [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son")[[note 2]](#cite_note-nontrinitarianism-9)—the [Logos](/wiki/Logos_(Christianity) "Logos (Christianity)") [incarnated](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)")—who [ministered](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus"), [suffered](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus"), and died on a cross, but rose from the dead for the [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") of humankind; this message is called [the gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel"), meaning the "good news". The four [canonical gospels](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark"), [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") and [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") describe Jesus' life and teachings as preserved in the early Christian tradition, with the Old Testament as the gospels' respected background.

Christianity [began in the 1st century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century"), after the death of Jesus, as a [Judaic](/wiki/Second_Temple_Judaism "Second Temple Judaism") sect with [Hellenistic influence](/wiki/Hellenistic_Judaism "Hellenistic Judaism") in the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") province of [Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)"). The [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)") of Jesus [spread their faith](/wiki/Spread_of_Christianity "Spread of Christianity") around the [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") area, despite [significant persecution](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Roman_Empire "Persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire"). The inclusion of [Gentiles](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile") led Christianity to slowly [separate from Judaism](/wiki/Split_of_Christianity_and_Judaism "Split of Christianity and Judaism") in the 2nd century. Emperor [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I "Constantine I") decriminalized Christianity in the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") by the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan") in 313 AD. Later, Constantine convened the [Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in 325 AD, where the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") was adopted, and where Early Christianity was consolidated into what would become the state religion of the Roman Empire by around 380 AD. The [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") and [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy") both split over differences in [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") during the 5th century, while the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") separated in the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") in the year 1054. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") split into numerous denominations from the Catholic Church during the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") era (16th century). Following the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") (15th–17th century), Christianity expanded throughout the world via [missionary work](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission"), [evangelism](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism"), immigration, and extensive trade. Christianity played [a prominent role](/wiki/Role_of_Christianity_in_civilization "Role of Christianity in civilization") in the [development](/wiki/Christian_ethics "Christian ethics") of [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_civilization "Western civilization"), particularly in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") from [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity") and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").

The three main [branches of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") are [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (1.3 billion people), [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (800 million),[[note 3]](#cite_note-11) and [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") (300 million), while other prominent branches include [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") (60 million) and [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") (35 million).[[note 4]](#cite_note-14) Smaller church communities number in the thousands. In Christianity, efforts toward unity ([ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism")) are underway.[[11]](#cite_note-Briggs1975-15)[[12]](#cite_note-Chia2018-16) In the [West](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), Christianity remains the dominant religion even with a [decline in adherence](/wiki/Decline_of_Christianity_in_the_Western_world "Decline of Christianity in the Western world"), with about 70% of that population identifying as Christian. [Christianity is growing](/wiki/Christian_population_growth "Christian population growth") in Africa and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), the world's most populous continents. Many Christians are still [persecuted](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians") in some regions of the world, particularly where they are a minority, such as in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"), [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia").

## Etymology

Further information: [Christ (title)](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")

Early Jewish Christians referred to themselves as being of 'The Way' ([Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek_language "Koine Greek language"): τῆς ὁδοῦ, romanized: *tês hodoû*), an expression possibly coming from [Isaiah 40:3](/wiki/Isaiah_40:3 "Isaiah 40:3"), "prepare the way of the Lord".[[note 5]](#cite_note-22) According to [Acts 11:26](/wiki/Acts_11:26 "Acts 11:26"), the term "Christian" (Χρῑστῐᾱνός, *Khrīstiānós*), meaning "followers of Christ" and referring to Jesus' [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)"), was first used in the city of [Antioch](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Antioch "Early centers of Christianity") by the non-Jewish inhabitants there.[[18]](#cite_note-23) The earliest recorded use of the term "Christianity/Christianism" (Χρῑστῐᾱνισμός, *Khrīstiānismós*) was by [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch") around 100 [AD](/wiki/Anno_Domini "Anno Domini").[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElwellComfort2001266,_828-24) The name Jesus comes from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἰησοῦς *Iēsous*, probably from [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew")/[Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic"): יֵשׁוּעַ *Yēšūaʿ.*

## History

Main article: [History of Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Christianity](/wiki/Timeline_of_Christianity "Timeline of Christianity").

### Early Christianity

Main article: [Early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity")

Further information: [Historical background of the New Testament](/wiki/Historical_background_of_the_New_Testament "Historical background of the New Testament")

#### Apostolic Age

Main article: [Christianity in the 1st century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_1st_century "Christianity in the 1st century")

Further information: [Chronology of Jesus](/wiki/Chronology_of_Jesus "Chronology of Jesus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/PikiWiki_Israel_73792_mount_zion_jerusalem.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_73792_mount_zion_jerusalem.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_73792_mount_zion_jerusalem.jpg)

The [Cenacle](/wiki/Cenacle "Cenacle") on [Mount Zion](/wiki/Mount_Zion "Mount Zion") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), claimed to be the location of the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")

Christianity developed during the 1st century AD in [Judaea](/wiki/Judaea_(Roman_province) "Judaea (Roman province)") as a [Jewish Christian](/wiki/Jewish_Christian "Jewish Christian") sect with [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_religion "Hellenistic religion") influence[[20]](#cite_note-25) of [Second Temple Judaism](/wiki/Second_Temple_Judaism "Second Temple Judaism").[[21]](#cite_note-26)[[22]](#cite_note-27) An early Jewish Christian community was founded in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") under the leadership of the three [Pillars of the Church](/wiki/Pillars_of_the_Church "Pillars of the Church"), namely [James the Just](/wiki/James_the_Just "James the Just"), [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter"), and [John](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle").[[23]](#cite_note-28)

Jewish Christianity soon attracted [Gentile](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile") [God-fearers](/wiki/God-fearer "God-fearer"), posing a problem for its [Jewish religious outlook](/wiki/Jewish_principles_of_faith "Jewish principles of faith"), which insisted on close observance of the Jewish commandments. [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") solved this by insisting that salvation by [faith in Christ](/wiki/Pistis_Christou "Pistis Christou"), and [participation](/wiki/Participation_in_Christ "Participation in Christ") in his death and resurrection by their baptism, sufficed.[[24]](#cite_note-29) At first he persecuted the early Christians, but after a conversion experience he preached to the gentiles, and is regarded as having had a formative effect on the emerging [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") identity as [separate from Judaism](/wiki/Split_of_Christianity_and_Judaism "Split of Christianity and Judaism"). Eventually, his departure from Jewish customs would result in the establishment of Christianity as an independent religion.[[25]](#cite_note-30)

#### Ante-Nicene period

Main article: [Christianity in the ante-Nicene period](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_ante-Nicene_period "Christianity in the ante-Nicene period")

Further information: [Great Church](/wiki/Great_Church "Great Church") and [Gnosticism](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Ann_Arbor%2C_University_of_Michigan_P.Mich.inv._6238_%28Papyrus_46%29_fol._142r_-_2_Corinthians_11%2C33-12%2C9.jpg/250px-Ann_Arbor%2C_University_of_Michigan_P.Mich.inv._6238_%28Papyrus_46%29_fol._142r_-_2_Corinthians_11%2C33-12%2C9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ann_Arbor,_University_of_Michigan_P.Mich.inv._6238_(Papyrus_46)_fol._142r_-_2_Corinthians_11,33-12,9.jpg)

A folio from [Papyrus 46](/wiki/Papyrus_46 "Papyrus 46"), an early-3rd-century collection of [Pauline epistles](/wiki/Pauline_epistles "Pauline epistles")

This formative period was followed by the early [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop"), whom Christians consider the [successors of Christ's apostles](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"). From the year 150, Christian teachers began to produce theological and apologetic works aimed at defending the faith. These authors are known as the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), and the study of them is called [patristics](/wiki/Patristics "Patristics"). Notable early Fathers include [Ignatius of Antioch](/wiki/Ignatius_of_Antioch "Ignatius of Antioch"), [Polycarp](/wiki/Polycarp "Polycarp"), [Justin Martyr](/wiki/Justin_Martyr "Justin Martyr"), [Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus"), [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"), [Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria") and [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen").

[Persecution of Christians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians") occurred intermittently and on a small scale by both Jewish and [Roman authorities](/wiki/Persecution_of_early_Christians_by_the_Romans "Persecution of early Christians by the Romans"), with Roman action starting at the time of the [Great Fire of Rome](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_Rome "Great Fire of Rome") in 64 AD. Examples of early executions under Jewish authority reported in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") include the deaths of [Saint Stephen](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen")[[26]](#cite_note-31) and [James, son of Zebedee](/wiki/James,_son_of_Zebedee "James, son of Zebedee").[[27]](#cite_note-32) The [Decian persecution](/wiki/Decian_persecution "Decian persecution") was the first empire-wide conflict,[[28]](#cite_note-33) when the edict of [Decius](/wiki/Decius "Decius") in 250 AD required everyone in the Roman Empire (except Jews) to perform a sacrifice to the Roman gods. The [Diocletianic Persecution](/wiki/Diocletianic_Persecution "Diocletianic Persecution") beginning in 303 AD was also particularly severe. Roman persecution ended in 313 AD with the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan").

While [Proto-orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Proto-orthodox_Christianity "Proto-orthodox Christianity") was becoming dominant, heterodox sects also existed at the same time, which held radically different beliefs. [Gnostic Christianity](/wiki/Gnostic_Christianity "Gnostic Christianity") developed a [duotheistic](/wiki/Duotheistic "Duotheistic") doctrine based on illusion and enlightenment rather than forgiveness of sin. With only a few scriptures overlapping with the developing orthodox canon, most [Gnostic texts](/wiki/Gnostic_texts "Gnostic texts") and [Gnostic gospels](/wiki/Gnostic_gospels "Gnostic gospels") were eventually considered heretical and suppressed by mainstream Christians. A gradual splitting off of [Gentile Christianity](/wiki/Gentile_Christianity "Gentile Christianity") left [Jewish Christians](/wiki/Jewish_Christians "Jewish Christians") continuing to follow the [Law of Moses](/wiki/Law_of_Moses "Law of Moses"), including practices such as circumcision. By the fifth century, they and the [Jewish–Christian gospels](/wiki/Jewish%E2%80%93Christian_gospels "Jewish–Christian gospels") would be largely suppressed by the dominant sects in both Judaism and Christianity.

### Spread and acceptance in the Roman Empire

Further information: [Christianity in late antiquity](/wiki/Christianity_in_late_antiquity "Christianity in late antiquity")

See also: [Historiography of the Christianization of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_Christianization_of_the_Roman_Empire "Historiography of the Christianization of the Roman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Mor-mattai.png/250px-Mor-mattai.png)](/wiki/File:Mor-mattai.png)

The [Monastery of St. Matthew](/wiki/Mar_Mattai_Monastery "Mar Mattai Monastery"), located atop [Mount Alfaf](/wiki/Mount_Alfaf "Mount Alfaf") in northern [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), is recognized as one of the oldest Christian monasteries in existence.[[29]](#cite_note-34)

Christianity spread to [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic")-speaking peoples along the [Mediterranean coast](/wiki/Mediterranean_coast "Mediterranean coast") and also to the inland parts of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and beyond that into the [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") and the later [Sasanian Empire](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"), including [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), which was dominated at different times and to varying extents by these empires.[[30]](#cite_note-35) The presence of Christianity in Africa began in the middle of the 1st century in Egypt and by the end of the 2nd century in the region around [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage"). [Mark the Evangelist](/wiki/Mark_the_Evangelist "Mark the Evangelist") is claimed to have started the [Church of Alexandria](/wiki/Church_of_Alexandria "Church of Alexandria") in about 43 AD; various later churches claim this as their own legacy, including the [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church").[[31]](#cite_note-36)[[32]](#cite_note-37)[[33]](#cite_note-38) Important Africans who influenced the early development of Christianity include [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian"), [Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria"), [Origen of Alexandria](/wiki/Origen_of_Alexandria "Origen of Alexandria"), [Cyprian](/wiki/Cyprian "Cyprian"), [Athanasius](/wiki/Athanasius "Athanasius"), and [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Khor_Virap_Monastery_and_Mount_Ararat%2C_Armenia.jpg/250px-Khor_Virap_Monastery_and_Mount_Ararat%2C_Armenia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khor_Virap_Monastery_and_Mount_Ararat,_Armenia.jpg)

The 7th-century [Khor Virap](/wiki/Khor_Virap "Khor Virap") monastery in the shadow of [Mount Ararat](/wiki/Mount_Ararat "Mount Ararat"); [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") was the first state to adopt Christianity as the state religion in the early 4th century AD.[[34]](#cite_note-CIA-39)[[35]](#cite_note-almanac-40)

[King Tiridates III](/wiki/Tiridates_III_of_Armenia "Tiridates III of Armenia") made Christianity the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") in [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") in the early 4th century AD, making Armenia the first officially Christian state.[[34]](#cite_note-CIA-39)[[35]](#cite_note-almanac-40) It was not an entirely new religion in Armenia, having penetrated into the country from at least the third century, but it may have been present even earlier.[[36]](#cite_note-41)

[Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") was exposed to Christianity in his youth, and throughout his life his support for the religion grew, culminating in baptism on his deathbed.[[37]](#cite_note-42) During his reign, state-sanctioned persecution of Christians was ended with the [Edict of Toleration](/wiki/Edict_of_Serdica "Edict of Serdica") in 311 and the [Edict of Milan](/wiki/Edict_of_Milan "Edict of Milan") in 313. At that point, Christianity was still a minority belief, comprising perhaps only 5% of the Roman population.[[38]](#cite_note-43) Influenced by his adviser [Mardonius](/wiki/Mardonius_(philosopher) "Mardonius (philosopher)"), Constantine's nephew [Julian](/wiki/Julian_(emperor) "Julian (emperor)") unsuccessfully tried to suppress Christianity.[[39]](#cite_note-44) On 27 February 380, [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I"), [Gratian](/wiki/Gratian "Gratian"), and [Valentinian II](/wiki/Valentinian_II "Valentinian II") issued the [Edict of Thessalonica](/wiki/Edict_of_Thessalonica "Edict of Thessalonica") establishing [Nicene Christianity](/wiki/Nicene_Christianity "Nicene Christianity") as the [State church of the Roman Empire](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire").[[40]](#cite_note-45) As soon as it became connected to the state, Christianity grew wealthy; the Church solicited donations from the rich and could now own land.[[41]](#cite_note-46)

Constantine was also instrumental in the convocation of the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") in 325, which sought to address [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") and formulated the Nicene Creed, which is still used by in [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and many other [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") churches.[[42]](#cite_note-47)[[43]](#cite_note-UMC—Our_Common_Heritage_as_Christians-48) Nicaea was the first of a series of [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council"), which formally defined critical elements of the theology of the Church, notably concerning [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology").[[44]](#cite_note-49) The [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") did not accept the third and following ecumenical councils and is still separate today by its successors ([Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East")).[[45]](#cite_note-50)

In terms of prosperity and cultural life, the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") was one of the peaks in [Christian history](/wiki/Christian_history "Christian history") and [Christian civilization](/wiki/Christian_civilization "Christian civilization"),[[46]](#cite_note-Cameron_2006_42-51) and [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") remained the leading city of the [Christian world](/wiki/Christian_world "Christian world") in size, wealth, and culture.[[47]](#cite_note-52) [There was a renewed interest in classical Greek philosophy](/wiki/Greek_scholars_in_the_Renaissance "Greek scholars in the Renaissance"), as well as an increase in literary output in vernacular Greek.[[48]](#cite_note-53) Byzantine art and literature held a preeminent place in Europe, and the cultural impact of [Byzantine art](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art") on the West during this period was enormous and of long-lasting significance.[[49]](#cite_note-54) The later rise of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") in North Africa reduced the size and numbers of Christian congregations, leaving in large numbers only the [Coptic Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church") in Egypt, the [Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Tewahedo_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church") in the Horn of Africa and the [Nubian Church](/wiki/Christianity_in_Sudan "Christianity in Sudan") in the Sudan (Nobatia, Makuria and Alodia).

### Middle Ages

Main article: [Christianity in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Middle_Ages "Christianity in the Middle Ages")

#### Early Middle Ages

With the decline and [fall of the Roman Empire in the West](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") became a political player, first visible in [Pope Leo](/wiki/Pope_Leo_I "Pope Leo I")'s diplomatic dealings with [Huns](/wiki/Attila_the_Hun "Attila the Hun") and [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals").[[50]](#cite_note-Gonzalez-p238-55) The church also entered into a long period of missionary activity and expansion among the various tribes. While [Arianists](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") instituted the death penalty for practicing pagans (see the [Massacre of Verden](/wiki/Massacre_of_Verden "Massacre of Verden"), for example), [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") also spread among the [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), the [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"),[[50]](#cite_note-Gonzalez-p238-55) the [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts"), the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_peoples "Baltic peoples") and some [Slavic peoples](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples").[[51]](#cite_note-56)

Around 500, Christianity was thoroughly integrated into Byzantine and [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom") culture[[52]](#cite_note-57) and [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia") set out [his Monastic Rule](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Benedict "Rule of Saint Benedict"), establishing a system of regulations for the foundation and running of [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries").[[50]](#cite_note-Gonzalez-p238-55) [Monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") became a powerful force throughout Europe,[[50]](#cite_note-Gonzalez-p238-55) and gave rise to many early centers of learning, most famously in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), and [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), contributing to the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance") of the 9th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a7/Krist_spred_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Krist_spred_3.jpg)

[Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") by AD 600 after its [spread to Africa and Europe](/wiki/Spread_of_Christianity "Spread of Christianity") from the Middle East

In the 7th century, [Muslims conquered Syria](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Syria "Muslim conquest of Syria") (including [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem")), North Africa, and Spain, converting some of the Christian population to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), including some of the [Christian populations in pre-Islamic Arabia](/wiki/Christianity_in_pre-Islamic_Arabia "Christianity in pre-Islamic Arabia"), and placing the rest under a separate [legal status](/wiki/Dhimmi "Dhimmi").[[53]](#cite_note-58)[[54]](#cite_note-Cahen-59) Part of the Muslims' success was due to the exhaustion of the Byzantine Empire in its decades long conflict with [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMullin200888-60) Beginning in the 8th century, with the rise of [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian "Carolingian") leaders, the Papacy sought greater political support in the [Frankish Kingdom](/wiki/Frankish_Kingdom "Frankish Kingdom").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMullin200893–94-61)

The Middle Ages brought about major changes within the church.[[57]](#cite_note-62)[[58]](#cite_note-63)[[59]](#cite_note-64)[[60]](#cite_note-65) [Pope Gregory the Great](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_the_Great "Pope Gregory the Great") dramatically reformed the [ecclesiastical structure](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_polity "Ecclesiastical polity") and administration.[[61]](#cite_note-66) In the early 8th century, [iconoclasm](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") became a divisive issue, when it was sponsored by the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") emperors. The [Second Ecumenical Council of Nicaea](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Nicaea "Second Council of Nicaea") (787) finally pronounced in favor of icons.[[62]](#cite_note-67) In the early 10th century, Western Christian monasticism was further rejuvenated through the leadership of the great Benedictine monastery of [Cluny](/wiki/Cluny_Abbey "Cluny Abbey").[[63]](#cite_note-68)

#### High and Late Middle Ages

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Jesus-Christ-from-Hagia-Sophia.jpg/250px-Jesus-Christ-from-Hagia-Sophia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jesus-Christ-from-Hagia-Sophia.jpg)

An example of Byzantine pictorial art, the [Deësis](/wiki/De%C3%ABsis "Deësis") mosaic at the [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia#Deësis_mosaic "Hagia Sophia") in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Passages_d%27outremer_Fr5594%2C_fol._19r%2C_Concile_de_Clermont.jpg/250px-Passages_d%27outremer_Fr5594%2C_fol._19r%2C_Concile_de_Clermont.jpg)](/wiki/File:Passages_d%27outremer_Fr5594,_fol._19r,_Concile_de_Clermont.jpg)

[Pope Urban II](/wiki/Pope_Urban_II "Pope Urban II") at the [Council of Clermont](/wiki/Council_of_Clermont "Council of Clermont"), where he preached the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade"). Illustration by [Jean Colombe](/wiki/Jean_Colombe "Jean Colombe") from the *[Passages d'outremer](/wiki/Passages_d%27outremer "Passages d'outremer")*, c. 1490.

In the West, from the 11th century onward, some older cathedral schools [became universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") (see, for example, [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), [University of Paris](/wiki/University_of_Paris "University of Paris") and [University of Bologna](/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna")). Previously, higher education had been the domain of Christian [cathedral schools](/wiki/Cathedral_school "Cathedral school") or [monastic schools](/wiki/Monastic_school "Monastic school") (*Scholae monasticae*), led by [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") and [nuns](/wiki/Nun "Nun"). Evidence of such schools dates back to the 6th century AD.[[64]](#cite_note-69) These new universities expanded the curriculum to include academic programs for clerics, lawyers, civil servants, and physicians.[[65]](#cite_note-70) The university is generally regarded as an institution that has its origin in the [Medieval Christian](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity") setting.[[66]](#cite_note-verger1999-71)[[67]](#cite_note-72)[[68]](#cite_note-73)

Accompanying the rise of the "new towns" throughout Europe, [mendicant orders](/wiki/Mendicant_order "Mendicant order") were founded, bringing the [consecrated religious life](/wiki/Consecrated_life_(Catholic_Church) "Consecrated life (Catholic Church)") out of the monastery and into the new urban setting. The two principal mendicant movements were the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans")[[69]](#cite_note-74) and the [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"),[[70]](#cite_note-75) founded by [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi") and [Dominic](/wiki/Saint_Dominic "Saint Dominic"), respectively. Both orders made significant contributions to the development of the great universities of Europe. Another new order was the [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians"), whose large, isolated monasteries spearheaded the settlement of former wilderness areas. In this period, church building and ecclesiastical architecture reached new heights, culminating in the orders of [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") and [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") and the building of the great European cathedrals.[[71]](#cite_note-76)

[Christian nationalism](/wiki/Christian_nationalism "Christian nationalism") emerged during this era in which Christians felt the desire to recover lands in which Christianity had historically flourished.[[72]](#cite_note-77) From 1095 under the pontificate of [Urban II](/wiki/Urban_II "Urban II"), the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") was launched.[[73]](#cite_note-78) These were a series of military campaigns in the [Holy Land](/wiki/Holy_Land "Holy Land") and elsewhere, initiated in response to pleas from the Byzantine Emperor [Alexios I](/wiki/Alexios_I "Alexios I") for aid against [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") expansion. The Crusades ultimately failed to stifle Islamic aggression and even contributed to Christian enmity with the sacking of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") during the [Fourth Crusade](/wiki/Fourth_Crusade "Fourth Crusade").[[74]](#cite_note-79)

The Christian Church experienced internal conflict between the 7th and 13th centuries that resulted in a [schism](/wiki/East-West_Schism "East-West Schism") between the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") branch, the now-Catholic Church, and an [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), largely Greek, branch (the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church")). The two sides disagreed on a number of administrative, liturgical and doctrinal issues, most prominently [Eastern Orthodox opposition to papal supremacy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_opposition_to_papal_supremacy "Eastern Orthodox opposition to papal supremacy").[[75]](#cite_note-80)[[76]](#cite_note-81) The [Second Council of Lyon](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Lyon "Second Council of Lyon") (1274) and the [Council of Florence](/wiki/Council_of_Florence "Council of Florence") (1439) attempted to reunite the churches, but in both cases, the Eastern Orthodox refused to implement the decisions, and the two principal churches remain in schism to the present day. However, the Catholic Church has achieved union with various [smaller eastern churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches").

In the thirteenth century, a new emphasis on Jesus' suffering, exemplified by the Franciscans' preaching, had the consequence of turning worshippers' attention towards Jews, on whom [Christians had placed the blame for Jesus' death](/wiki/Jewish_deicide "Jewish deicide"). Christianity's limited tolerance of Jews was not new—Augustine of Hippo said that Jews should not be allowed to enjoy the citizenship that Christians took for granted—but the growing antipathy towards Jews was a factor that led to [the expulsion of Jews from England in 1290](/wiki/Edict_of_Expulsion "Edict of Expulsion"), the first of many such expulsions in Europe.[[77]](#cite_note-82)[[78]](#cite_note-83)

Beginning around 1184, following the crusade against [Cathar](/wiki/Cathars "Cathars") heresy,[[79]](#cite_note-84) various institutions, broadly referred to as the [Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition"), were established with the aim of suppressing [heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy") and securing religious and doctrinal unity within Christianity through [conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion") and prosecution.[[80]](#cite_note-85)

### Modern era

Main article: [Christianity in the modern era](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_modern_era "Christianity in the modern era")

#### Protestant Reformation and Counter-Reformation

Main articles: [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") and [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation")

See also: [European wars of religion](/wiki/European_wars_of_religion "European wars of religion") and [Renaissance Papacy](/wiki/Renaissance_Papacy "Renaissance Papacy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Luther_95_Thesen.png/250px-Luther_95_Thesen.png)](/wiki/File:Luther_95_Thesen.png)

[Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") initiated the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation") with his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* in 1517.

The 15th-century [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") brought about a renewed interest in ancient and classical learning. During the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") posted the *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* 1517 against the sale of [indulgences](/wiki/Indulgences "Indulgences").[[81]](#cite_note-Simon-86) Printed copies soon spread throughout Europe. In 1521 the [Edict of Worms](/wiki/Edict_of_Worms "Edict of Worms") condemned and excommunicated Luther and his followers, resulting in the schism of the [Western Christendom](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") into several branches.[[82]](#cite_note-87)

Other reformers like [Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"), [Oecolampadius](/wiki/Johannes_Oecolampadius "Johannes Oecolampadius"), [Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"), [Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox"), and [Arminius](/wiki/Jacobus_Arminius "Jacobus Arminius") further criticized Catholic teaching and worship. These challenges developed into the movement called [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), which repudiated the [primacy of the pope](/wiki/Papal_primacy "Papal primacy"), the role of tradition, the [seven sacraments](/wiki/Catholic_sacraments "Catholic sacraments"), and other doctrines and practices.[[81]](#cite_note-Simon-86) The [Reformation in England](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation") began in 1534, when [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") had himself [declared head](/wiki/Act_of_Supremacy "Act of Supremacy") of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). Beginning in 1536, the monasteries throughout England, Wales and Ireland were [dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_monasteries "Dissolution of the monasteries").[[83]](#cite_note-88)

[Thomas Müntzer](/wiki/Thomas_M%C3%BCntzer "Thomas Müntzer"), [Andreas Karlstadt](/wiki/Andreas_Karlstadt "Andreas Karlstadt") and other theologians perceived both the Catholic Church and the confessions of the [Magisterial Reformation](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation") as corrupted. Their activity brought about the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation"), which gave birth to various [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism") denominations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Michelangelo%27s_Pieta_5450_cropncleaned_edit.jpg/250px-Michelangelo%27s_Pieta_5450_cropncleaned_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michelangelo%27s_Pieta_5450_cropncleaned_edit.jpg)

[Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo")'s 1498–99 *[Pietà](/wiki/Michelangelo%27s_Piet%C3%A0 "Michelangelo's Pietà")* in [St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica"); the Catholic Church was among the patronages of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance").[[84]](#cite_note-89)[[85]](#cite_note-90)[[86]](#cite_note-91)

Partly in response to the Protestant Reformation, the Catholic Church engaged in a substantial process of reform and renewal, known as the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") or Catholic Reform.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBokenkotter2004242–244-92) The [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") clarified and reasserted Catholic doctrine. During the following centuries, competition between Catholicism and Protestantism became deeply entangled with political struggles among European states.[[88]](#cite_note-93)

Meanwhile, the discovery of America by [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in 1492 brought about a new wave of missionary activity. Partly from missionary zeal, but under the impetus of [colonial expansion](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") by the European powers, Christianity spread to the Americas, Oceania, East Asia and sub-Saharan Africa.

Throughout Europe, the division caused by the Reformation led to outbreaks of [religious violence](/wiki/Religious_violence "Religious violence") and the establishment of separate state churches in Europe. [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") spread into the northern, central, and eastern parts of present-day Germany, [Livonia](/wiki/Livonia "Livonia"), and Scandinavia. [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") was established in England in 1534. [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") and its varieties, such as [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), were introduced in Scotland, the Netherlands, Hungary, Switzerland, and France. [Arminianism](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") gained followers in the Netherlands and [Frisia](/wiki/Frisia "Frisia"). Ultimately, these differences led to the outbreak of [conflicts](/wiki/Religious_war "Religious war") in which religion played a key factor. The [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), and the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion") are prominent examples. These events intensified the [Christian debate on persecution and toleration](/wiki/Christian_debate_on_persecution_and_toleration "Christian debate on persecution and toleration").[[89]](#cite_note-94)

In the revival of neoplatonism [Renaissance humanists](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") did not reject Christianity; quite the contrary, many of the greatest works of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") were devoted to it, and the Catholic Church patronized many works of [Renaissance art](/wiki/Renaissance_art "Renaissance art").[[90]](#cite_note-openuni-95) Much, if not most, of the new art was commissioned by or in dedication to the Church.[[90]](#cite_note-openuni-95) Some scholars and historians attribute Christianity to having contributed to the rise of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution").[[91]](#cite_note-96) Many well-known historical figures who influenced [Western science](/wiki/Western_science "Western science") considered themselves Christian such as [Nicolaus Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus"),[[92]](#cite_note-97) [Galileo Galilei](/wiki/Galileo_Galilei "Galileo Galilei"),[[93]](#cite_note-98) [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler"),[[94]](#cite_note-99) [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton")[[95]](#cite_note-100) and [Robert Boyle](/wiki/Robert_Boyle "Robert Boyle").[[96]](#cite_note-101)

#### Post-Enlightenment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Madonna_and_Child%2C_Kakure_Kirishitan.jpg/250px-Madonna_and_Child%2C_Kakure_Kirishitan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madonna_and_Child,_Kakure_Kirishitan.jpg)

A depiction of [Madonna and Child](/wiki/Madonna_and_Child "Madonna and Child") in a 19th-century [Kakure Kirishitan](/wiki/Kakure_Kirishitan "Kakure Kirishitan") [Japanese woodcut](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e")

In the era known as the [Great Divergence](/wiki/Great_Divergence "Great Divergence"), when in the West, the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution") brought about great societal changes, Christianity was confronted with various forms of [skepticism](/wiki/Skepticism "Skepticism") and with certain modern [political ideologies](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology"), such as versions of [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") and [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism").[[97]](#cite_note-102) Events ranged from mere [anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") to violent outbursts against Christianity, such as the [dechristianization of France during the French Revolution](/wiki/Dechristianisation_of_France_during_the_French_Revolution "Dechristianisation of France during the French Revolution"),[[98]](#cite_note-103) the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), and certain [Marxist](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") movements, especially [the Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_(1917) "Russian Revolution (1917)") and the [persecution of Christians in the Soviet Union](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Soviet_Union "Persecution of Christians in the Soviet Union") under [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism").[[99]](#cite_note-104)[[100]](#cite_note-105)[[101]](#cite_note-106)[[102]](#cite_note-107)

Especially pressing in Europe was the formation of [nation states](/wiki/Nation_states "Nation states") after the [Napoleonic era](/wiki/Napoleonic_era "Napoleonic era"). In all European countries, different Christian denominations found themselves in competition to greater or lesser extents with each other and with the state. Variables were the relative sizes of the denominations and the religious, political, and ideological orientation of the states. Urs Altermatt of the [University of Fribourg](/wiki/University_of_Fribourg "University of Fribourg"), looking specifically at Catholicism in Europe, identifies four models for the European nations. In traditionally Catholic-majority countries such as Belgium, Spain, and Austria, to some extent, religious and national communities are more or less identical. Cultural symbiosis and separation are found in Poland, the Republic of Ireland, and Switzerland, all countries with competing denominations. Competition is found in Germany, the Netherlands, and again Switzerland, all countries with minority Catholic populations, which to a greater or lesser extent identified with the nation. Finally, separation between religion (again, specifically Catholicism) and the state is found to a great degree in France and Italy, countries where the state actively opposed itself to the authority of the Catholic Church.[[103]](#cite_note-108)

The combined factors of the formation of nation states and [ultramontanism](/wiki/Ultramontanism "Ultramontanism"), especially in Germany and the Netherlands, but also in England to a much lesser extent,[[104]](#cite_note-109) often forced Catholic churches, organizations, and believers to choose between the national demands of the state and the authority of the Church, specifically the papacy. This conflict came to a head in the [First Vatican Council](/wiki/First_Vatican_Council "First Vatican Council"), and in Germany would lead directly to the *[Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")*.[[105]](#cite_note-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Consecration_of_new_pastors.jpg/250px-Consecration_of_new_pastors.jpg)](/wiki/File:Consecration_of_new_pastors.jpg)

[Ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination") of new pastors in [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), 2014

Christian commitment in Europe dropped as modernity and secularism came into their own,[[106]](#cite_note-111) particularly in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"),[[107]](#cite_note-112) while religious commitments in America have been generally high in comparison to Europe. Changes in worldwide Christianity over the last century have been significant, since 1900, Christianity has spread rapidly in the [Global South](/wiki/Global_South "Global South") and Third World countries.[[108]](#cite_note-113) The late 20th century has shown the shift of Christian adherence to the [Third World](/wiki/Third_World "Third World") and the Southern Hemisphere in general,[[109]](#cite_note-114)[[110]](#cite_note-115) with the West no longer the chief standard bearer of Christianity. Approximately 7 to 10% of [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") are [Christians](/wiki/Arab_Christians_and_Arabic-speaking_Christians "Arab Christians and Arabic-speaking Christians"),[[111]](#cite_note-116) most prevalent in Egypt, [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon").[[112]](#cite_note-117)

## Beliefs

While Christians worldwide share basic convictions, there are differences of interpretations and opinions of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") and [sacred traditions](/wiki/Sacred_tradition "Sacred tradition") on which Christianity is based.[[113]](#cite_note-118) Christianity encompasses various movements of [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology"), the main ones being conservative [fundamentalist](/wiki/Christian_fundamentalism "Christian fundamentalism") or [moderate](/wiki/Moderate_Christianity "Moderate Christianity"), [liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") and [progressive](/wiki/Progressive_Christianity "Progressive Christianity").[[114]](#cite_note-119)

### Creeds

Main articles: [Christian creed](/wiki/Christian_creed "Christian creed") and [List of Christian creeds](/wiki/List_of_Christian_creeds "List of Christian creeds")

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Apostles' Creed](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Apostles%27_Creed "s:en:Apostles' Creed")**

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Nicene Creed](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Nicene_Creed "s:en:Nicene Creed")**

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Nicaea_icon.jpg/250px-Nicaea_icon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicaea_icon.jpg)

An [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") icon depicting [Emperor Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") and the Fathers of the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") (325) as holding the Niceno–Constantinopolitan Creed of 381

Concise doctrinal statements or confessions of religious beliefs are known as [creeds](/wiki/Creeds "Creeds"). They began as baptismal formulae and were later expanded during the [Christological](/wiki/Christology "Christology") controversies of the 4th and 5th centuries to become statements of faith. "[Jesus is Lord](/wiki/Jesus_is_Lord "Jesus is Lord")" is the earliest creed of Christianity and continues to be used, as with the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches").[[115]](#cite_note-120)

The [Apostles' Creed](/wiki/Apostles%27_Creed "Apostles' Creed") is the most widely accepted statement of the articles of Christian faith. It is used by a number of [Christian denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations") for both [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and [catechetical](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis") purposes, most visibly by liturgical churches of [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian "Western Christian") tradition, including the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), and [Western Rite Orthodoxy](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy"). It is also used by [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), [Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), and [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church").

This particular creed was developed between the 2nd and 9th centuries. Its central doctrines are those of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") and [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") the [Creator](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity"). Each of the doctrines found in this creed can be traced to statements current in the [apostolic period](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age"). The creed was apparently used as a summary of Christian doctrine for baptismal candidates in the churches of Rome.[[116]](#cite_note-121) Its points include:

* Belief in [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") as the [Son of God](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son"),[[117]](#cite_note-122)[[118]](#cite_note-123)[[119]](#cite_note-124)[[note 2]](#cite_note-nontrinitarianism-9) and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)")
* The [death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), [descent into hell](/wiki/Harrowing_of_Hell "Harrowing of Hell"), [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") and [ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") of Christ
* The holiness of the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") and the [communion of saints](/wiki/Communion_of_saints "Communion of saints")
* Christ's [second coming](/wiki/Second_coming "Second coming"), the [Day of Judgement](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment") and [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity") of the faithful

The [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") was formulated, largely in response to [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism"), at the Councils of [Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") and [Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") in 325 and 381 respectively,[[120]](#cite_note-125)[[121]](#cite_note-126) and ratified as the universal creed of [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") by the [First Council of Ephesus](/wiki/First_Council_of_Ephesus "First Council of Ephesus") in 431.[[122]](#cite_note-127)

The [Chalcedonian Definition](/wiki/Chalcedonian_Definition "Chalcedonian Definition"), or Creed of Chalcedon, developed at the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") in 451,[[123]](#cite_note-128) though rejected by the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox"),[[124]](#cite_note-129) taught Christ "to be acknowledged in two natures, inconfusedly, unchangeably, indivisibly, inseparably": one divine and one human, and that both natures, while perfect in themselves, are nevertheless also [perfectly united into one person](/wiki/Hypostatic_union "Hypostatic union").[[125]](#cite_note-130)

The [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed"), received in the Western Church as having the same status as the Nicene and Chalcedonian, says: "We worship one God in Trinity, and Trinity in Unity; neither confounding the Persons nor dividing the [Substance](/wiki/Consubstantiality "Consubstantiality")".[[126]](#cite_note-131)

Most Christians ([Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox"), and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") alike) accept the use of creeds and subscribe to at least one of the creeds mentioned above.[[43]](#cite_note-UMC—Our_Common_Heritage_as_Christians-48)

Certain [Evangelical Protestants](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestants "Evangelical Protestants"), though not all of them, reject creeds as definitive statements of faith, even while agreeing with some or all of the substance of the creeds. Also rejecting creeds are groups with roots in the [Restoration Movement](/wiki/Restoration_Movement "Restoration Movement"), such as the [Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)"), the [Evangelical Christian Church in Canada](/wiki/Evangelical_Christian_Church_in_Canada "Evangelical Christian Church in Canada"), and the [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ").[[127]](#cite_note-132)[[128]](#cite_note-133): 14–15[[129]](#cite_note-134): 123

### Jesus

Main articles: [Jesus in Christianity](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") and [Christ (title)](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")

See also: [Incarnation (Christianity)](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") and [Jesus in comparative mythology](/wiki/Jesus_in_comparative_mythology "Jesus in comparative mythology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/CompositeJesus.JPG/250px-CompositeJesus.JPG)](/wiki/File:CompositeJesus.JPG)

Various [depictions of Jesus](/wiki/Depiction_of_Jesus "Depiction of Jesus")

The central tenet of Christianity is the belief in [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") as the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(Christianity) "Son of God (Christianity)")[[note 2]](#cite_note-nontrinitarianism-9) and the [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") (Christ).[[130]](#cite_note-135)[[131]](#cite_note-136) Christians believe that Jesus, as the Messiah, was [anointed](/wiki/Anointing "Anointing") by God as savior of humanity and hold that Jesus' coming was the fulfillment of [messianic prophecies](/wiki/Christian_messianic_prophecies "Christian messianic prophecies") of the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"). The Christian concept of messiah differs significantly from [the contemporary Jewish concept](/wiki/Messiah_in_Judaism "Messiah in Judaism"). The core Christian belief is that through belief in and acceptance of [the death and resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Death_and_Resurrection_of_Jesus "Death and Resurrection of Jesus"), [sinful](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin") humans can be reconciled to God, and thereby are offered salvation and the promise of [eternal life](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality").[[132]](#cite_note-137)

While there have been many [theological](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") disputes over the [nature of Jesus](/wiki/Christology "Christology") over the earliest centuries of Christian history, generally, Christians believe that Jesus is [God incarnate](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") and "[true God and true man](/wiki/Hypostatic_union "Hypostatic union")" (or both fully divine and fully human). Jesus, having become [fully human](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)"), suffered the pains and temptations of a mortal man, but did not [sin](/wiki/Christian_views_on_sin "Christian views on sin"). As fully God, he rose to life again. According to the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), he [rose](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") from the dead,[[133]](#cite_note-138) [ascended](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") to heaven, is seated at the right hand of the Father,[[134]](#cite_note-139) and will ultimately [return](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming")[[135]](#cite_note-140) to fulfill the rest of the [Messianic prophecy](/wiki/Messianic_prophecy "Messianic prophecy"), including the [resurrection of the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_dead "Resurrection of the dead"), the [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment"), and the final establishment of the [Kingdom of God](/wiki/Kingdom_of_God_(Christianity) "Kingdom of God (Christianity)").

According to the [canonical gospels](/wiki/Canonical_gospels "Canonical gospels") of [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), Jesus was [conceived](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)") by the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)") and [born](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") from [the Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"). Little of Jesus' childhood is recorded in the canonical gospels, although [infancy gospels](/wiki/Infancy_gospels "Infancy gospels") were popular in antiquity.[[136]](#cite_note-141) In comparison, his adulthood, especially the week before his death, is well documented in the gospels contained within the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), because that part of his life is believed to be most important. The biblical accounts of [Jesus' ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") include: [his baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus"), [miracles](/wiki/Miracles_of_Jesus "Miracles of Jesus"), preaching, teaching, and deeds.

#### Death and resurrection

Main articles: [Crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") and [Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Cristo_crucificado.jpg/250px-Cristo_crucificado.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cristo_crucificado.jpg)

*[Crucifixion](/wiki/Christ_Crucified_(Vel%C3%A1zquez) "Christ Crucified (Velázquez)")*, representing the death of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") on the [Cross](/wiki/Christian_Cross "Christian Cross"), painting by [Diego Velázquez](/wiki/Diego_Vel%C3%A1zquez "Diego Velázquez"), c. 1632

Christians consider the resurrection of Jesus to be the cornerstone of their faith (see [1 Corinthians 15](/wiki/1_Corinthians_15 "1 Corinthians 15")).[[137]](#cite_note-142) Among Christian beliefs, the death and resurrection of Jesus are two core events on which much of Christian doctrine and theology is based.[[138]](#cite_note-143) According to the New Testament, Jesus was [crucified](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion"), died a physical death, was buried within a tomb, and rose from the dead three days later.[[139]](#cite_note-144)

The [New Testament mentions](/wiki/Overview_of_resurrection_appearances_in_the_Gospels_and_Paul "Overview of resurrection appearances in the Gospels and Paul") several [post-resurrection appearances of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus#Biblical_accounts "Resurrection of Jesus") on different occasions to his [twelve apostles](/wiki/Twelve_apostles "Twelve apostles") and [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)"), including "more than five hundred brethren at once",[[140]](#cite_note-145) before Jesus' [ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus") to heaven. Jesus' death and resurrection are commemorated by Christians in all worship services, with special emphasis during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), which includes [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") Sunday.

The death and resurrection of Jesus are usually considered the most important events in [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology"), partly because they demonstrate that Jesus has power over life and death and therefore has the authority and power to give people [eternal life](/wiki/Eternal_life_(Christianity) "Eternal life (Christianity)").[[141]](#cite_note-146)

Christian churches accept and teach the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") account of the resurrection of Jesus with very few exceptions.[[142]](#cite_note-147) Some modern scholars use the belief of Jesus' followers in the resurrection as a point of departure for establishing the continuity of the [historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus") and the proclamation of the [early church](/wiki/Early_church "Early church").[[143]](#cite_note-148) Some [liberal Christians](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") do not accept a literal bodily resurrection,[[144]](#cite_note-149)[[145]](#cite_note-150) seeing the story as richly symbolic and spiritually nourishing [myth](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"). Arguments over death and resurrection claims occur at many religious [debates](/wiki/Debate "Debate") and [interfaith dialogues](/wiki/Interfaith_dialogue "Interfaith dialogue").[[146]](#cite_note-151) [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"), an early Christian convert and missionary, wrote, "If Christ was not raised, then all our preaching is useless, and your trust in God is useless".[[147]](#cite_note-152)[[148]](#cite_note-153)

### Salvation

Main article: [Salvation in Christianity](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity")

> "For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life".

— John 3:16, NIV[[149]](#cite_note-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Lucas_Cranach_%28I%29_-_The_Law_and_the_Gospel.jpg/250px-Lucas_Cranach_%28I%29_-_The_Law_and_the_Gospel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lucas_Cranach_(I)_-_The_Law_and_the_Gospel.jpg)

*[The Law and the Gospel](/wiki/Law_and_Gospel_(Cranach) "Law and Gospel (Cranach)")* by Lucas Cranach the Elder (1529); Moses and Elijah point the sinner to Jesus for salvation.

[Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"), like Jews and Roman [pagans](/wiki/Pagan "Pagan") of his time, believed that sacrifice can bring about new kinship ties, purity, and eternal life.[[150]](#cite_note-remedy-155) For Paul, the necessary sacrifice was the death of Jesus: Gentiles who are "Christ's" are, like Israel, descendants of Abraham and "heirs according to the promise"[[151]](#cite_note-156)[[152]](#cite_note-157) The God who raised Jesus from the dead would also give new life to the "mortal bodies" of Gentile Christians, who had become with Israel, the "children of God", and were therefore no longer "in the flesh".[[153]](#cite_note-158)[[150]](#cite_note-remedy-155)

Modern Christian churches tend to be much more concerned with how humanity can be [saved](/wiki/Eternal_salvation "Eternal salvation") from a universal condition of sin and death than the question of how both Jews and Gentiles can be in God's family. According to [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") theology, based upon their understanding of the atonement as put forward by Irenaeus' [recapitulation theory](/wiki/Recapitulation_(Irenaeus) "Recapitulation (Irenaeus)"), Jesus' death is a [ransom](/wiki/Ransom_theory_of_atonement "Ransom theory of atonement"). This restores the relation with God, who is loving and reaches out to humanity, and offers the possibility of *[theosis](/wiki/Theosis_(Eastern_Orthodox_theology) "Theosis (Eastern Orthodox theology)")* c.q. [divinization](/wiki/Divinization_(Christian) "Divinization (Christian)"), becoming the kind of humans God wants humanity to be. The Catholic church also holds to a form of ransom theory[[154]](#cite_note-159) but also teaches that Jesus' death [satisfies](/wiki/Satisfaction_theory_of_atonement "Satisfaction theory of atonement") the debt to the Father incurred by the sins of humanity.[[155]](#cite_note-160) The Catholic Church further affirms that salvation does not occur without faithfulness on the part of Christians; converts must live in accordance with principles of love and ordinarily must be baptized.[[156]](#cite_note-161) In Protestant theology, Jesus' death is regarded as a [substitutionary penalty](/wiki/Penal_substitution "Penal substitution") carried by Jesus, for the debt that has to be paid by humankind when it broke God's moral law.[[157]](#cite_note-162)

Christians differ in their views on the extent to which individuals' salvation is pre-ordained by God. Reformed theology places distinctive emphasis on grace by teaching that individuals are [completely incapable of self-redemption](/wiki/Total_depravity "Total depravity"), but that [sanctifying grace is irresistible](/wiki/Irresistible_grace "Irresistible grace").[[158]](#cite_note-163) In contrast [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics"), Orthodox Christians, and [Arminian](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") Protestants believe that the exercise of [free will](/wiki/Free_will "Free will") is necessary to have faith in Jesus.[[159]](#cite_note-164)

### Trinity

Main article: [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg/250px-Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Shield-Trinity-Scutum-Fidei-English.svg)

The [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") is the belief that [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") is one God in three persons: the [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father"), the [Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") ([Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus")), and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_(Christianity) "Holy Spirit (Christianity)").[[160]](#cite_note-165)

*Trinity* refers to the teaching that the one God[[161]](#cite_note-166) comprises three distinct, eternally co-existing persons: the *[Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father")*, the *Son* (incarnate in Jesus Christ) and the *[Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity")*. Together, these three persons are sometimes called the [Godhead](/wiki/Godhead_(Christianity) "Godhead (Christianity)"),[[162]](#cite_note-167)[[163]](#cite_note-168)[[164]](#cite_note-169) although there is no single term in use in Scripture to denote the unified Godhead.[[165]](#cite_note-170) In the words of the [Athanasian Creed](/wiki/Athanasian_Creed "Athanasian Creed"), an early statement of Christian belief, "the Father is God, the Son is God, and the Holy Spirit is God, and yet there are not three Gods but one God".[[166]](#cite_note-171) They are distinct from another: the Father has no source, the Son is begotten of the Father, and the Spirit proceeds from the Father. Though distinct, the three persons cannot be divided from one another in being or in operation. While some Christians also believe that God appeared as the Father in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"), it is agreed that he appeared as the Son in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") and will still continue to manifest as the Holy Spirit in the present. But still, God still existed as three persons in each of these times.[[167]](#cite_note-172) However, traditionally there is a belief that it was the Son who appeared in the Old Testament because, for example, when the [Trinity is depicted in art](/wiki/Trinity#Art "Trinity"), the Son typically has the distinctive appearance, a [cruciform halo](/wiki/Cruciform_halo "Cruciform halo") identifying Christ, and in depictions of the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden"), this looks forward to an Incarnation yet to occur. In some [Early Christian](/wiki/Early_Christian_art "Early Christian art") [sarcophagi](/wiki/Sarcophagi "Sarcophagi"), the Logos is distinguished with a beard, "which allows him to appear ancient, even pre-existent".[[168]](#cite_note-173)

The [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") is an essential doctrine of mainstream Christianity. From earlier than the times of the Nicene Creed (325) Christianity advocated[[169]](#cite_note-174) the triune [mystery](/wiki/Holy_Mystery "Holy Mystery")-nature of [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") as a normative profession of faith. According to [Roger E. Olson](/wiki/Roger_E._Olson "Roger E. Olson") and Christopher Hall, through prayer, meditation, study and practice, the Christian community concluded "that God must exist as both a unity and trinity", codifying this in ecumenical council at the end of the 4th century.[[170]](#cite_note-175)[[171]](#cite_note-176)

According to this doctrine, God is not divided in the sense that each person has a third of the whole; rather, each person is considered to be fully God (see [Perichoresis](/wiki/Perichoresis "Perichoresis")). The distinction lies in their relations, the Father being unbegotten; the Son being begotten of the Father; and the Holy Spirit proceeding from the Father and (in [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian "Western Christian") theology) [from the Son](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque"). Regardless of this apparent difference, the three "persons" are each [eternal](/wiki/Eternity "Eternity") and [omnipotent](/wiki/Omnipotent "Omnipotent"). Other Christian religions including [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism"), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses"), and [Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism"), do not share those views on the Trinity.

The Greek word *trias*[[172]](#cite_note-177)[[note 6]](#cite_note-Trias-Trinitas-181) is first seen in this sense in the works of [Theophilus of Antioch](/wiki/Theophilus_of_Antioch "Theophilus of Antioch"); his text reads: "of the Trinity, of God, and of His Word, and of His Wisdom".[[176]](#cite_note-182) The term may have been in use before this time; its Latin equivalent,[[note 6]](#cite_note-Trias-Trinitas-181) *trinitas*,[[174]](#cite_note-Trinitas-179) appears afterwards with an explicit reference to the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, in [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian").[[177]](#cite_note-183)[[178]](#cite_note-184) In the following century, the word was in general use. It is found in many passages of [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen").[[179]](#cite_note-185)

#### Trinitarianism

Main article: [Trinitarianism](/wiki/Trinitarianism "Trinitarianism")

*Trinitarianism* denotes Christians who believe in the concept of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"). Almost all Christian denominations and churches hold Trinitarian beliefs. Although the words "Trinity" and "Triune" do not appear in the Bible, beginning in the 3rd century theologians developed the term and concept to facilitate [apprehension](/wiki/Apprehension_(understanding) "Apprehension (understanding)") of the New Testament teachings of God as being Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Since that time, Christian theologians have been careful to emphasize that Trinity does not imply that there are three gods (the antitrinitarian heresy of [Tritheism](/wiki/Tritheism "Tritheism")), nor that each hypostasis of the Trinity is one-third of an infinite God (partialism), nor that the Son and the Holy Spirit are beings created by and subordinate to the Father ([Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism")). Rather, the Trinity is defined as one God in three persons.[[180]](#cite_note-186)

#### Nontrinitarianism

Main article: [Nontrinitarianism](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism")

*Nontrinitarianism* (or *antitrinitarianism*) refers to theology that rejects the doctrine of the Trinity. Various nontrinitarian views, such as [adoptionism](/wiki/Adoptionism "Adoptionism") or [modalism](/wiki/Modalism "Modalism"), existed in early Christianity, leading to disputes about [Christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology").[[181]](#cite_note-187) Nontrinitarianism reappeared in the [Gnosticism](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism") of the [Cathars](/wiki/Cathars "Cathars") between the 11th and 13th centuries, among groups with [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") theology in the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") of the 16th century,[[182]](#cite_note-188) in the 18th-century [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), among [Restorationist](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") groups arising during the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening") of the 19th century, and most recently, in [Oneness Pentecostal](/wiki/Oneness_Pentecostalism "Oneness Pentecostalism") churches.

### Eschatology

Main article: [Christian eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology")

The end of things, whether the end of an individual life, the end of the age, or the end of the world, broadly speaking, is Christian eschatology; the study of the destiny of humans as it is revealed in the Bible. The major issues in Christian eschatology are the [Tribulation](/wiki/Great_Tribulation "Great Tribulation"), death and the afterlife, (mainly for [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity") groups) [the Millennium](/wiki/Millennialism "Millennialism") and the following [Rapture](/wiki/Rapture "Rapture"), the [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming") of Jesus, [Resurrection of the Dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_Dead "Resurrection of the Dead"), Heaven, (for [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") branches) [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"), and Hell, the [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment"), the end of the world, and the [New Heavens and New Earth](/wiki/New_Heavens_and_New_Earth "New Heavens and New Earth").

Christians believe that the second coming of Christ will occur at the [end of time](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology"), after a period of severe persecution (the Great Tribulation). All who have died will be [resurrected bodily from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_dead "Resurrection of the dead") for the Last Judgment. Jesus will fully establish the [Kingdom of God](/wiki/Kingdom_of_God "Kingdom of God") in fulfillment of [scriptural prophecies](/wiki/Bible_prophecy "Bible prophecy").[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)

#### Death and afterlife

Most Christians believe that human beings experience divine judgment and are rewarded either with eternal life or [eternal damnation](/wiki/Hell "Hell"). This includes the [general judgement](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment") at the [resurrection of the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_dead "Resurrection of the dead") as well as the belief (held by Catholics,[[185]](#cite_note-191)[[186]](#cite_note-192) Orthodox[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-194) and most Protestants) in a [judgment particular to the individual soul](/wiki/Particular_judgment "Particular judgment") after physical death.
