### Catholic Church

Main article: [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Pope_Leo_XIV_3_%283x4_cropped%29.png/250px-Pope_Leo_XIV_3_%283x4_cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Pope_Leo_XIV_3_(3x4_cropped).png)

[Pope Leo XIV](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIV "Pope Leo XIV"), the leader of the Catholic Church since 2025

The Catholic Church consists of those [particular Churches](/wiki/Particular_Church "Particular Church"), headed by bishops, in communion with the [pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), the bishop of Rome, as its highest authority in matters of faith, morality, and church governance.[[378]](#cite_note-388)[[379]](#cite_note-389) Like [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox"), the Catholic Church, through [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession"), traces its origins to the Christian community founded by Jesus Christ.[[380]](#cite_note-390)[[381]](#cite_note-391) Catholics maintain that the "[one, holy, catholic, and apostolic church](/wiki/Four_Marks_of_the_Church "Four Marks of the Church")" founded by Jesus [subsists fully](/wiki/Subsistit_in "Subsistit in") in the Catholic Church, but also acknowledges other Christian churches and communities[[382]](#cite_note-LumenGentium-392)[[383]](#cite_note-393) and works towards [reconciliation](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") among all Christians.[[382]](#cite_note-LumenGentium-392) The Catholic faith is detailed in the *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")*.[[384]](#cite_note-394)[[385]](#cite_note-395)

Of its [seven sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church"), the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist_in_the_Catholic_Church "Eucharist in the Catholic Church") is the principal one, celebrated [liturgically](/wiki/Catholic_liturgy "Catholic liturgy") in the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_in_the_Catholic_Church "Mass in the Catholic Church").[[386]](#cite_note-396) The church teaches that through [consecration](/wiki/Consecration#Eucharist "Consecration") by a [priest](/wiki/Priesthood_(Catholic_Church) "Priesthood (Catholic Church)"), the sacrificial [bread](/wiki/Sacramental_bread "Sacramental bread") and [wine](/wiki/Sacramental_wine "Sacramental wine") [become the body and blood of Christ](/wiki/Transubstantiation "Transubstantiation"). The [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") is [venerated](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church") in the Catholic Church as [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God") and [Queen of Heaven](/wiki/Queen_of_Heaven "Queen of Heaven"), honoured in [dogmas](/wiki/Mariology_of_the_Catholic_Church#Dogmatic_teachings "Mariology of the Catholic Church") and [devotions](/wiki/Marian_devotions "Marian devotions").[[387]](#cite_note-marian_dogmas-397) Its teaching includes [Divine Mercy](/wiki/Divine_Mercy "Divine Mercy"), [sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification "Sanctification") through faith and [evangelization](/wiki/Evangelization "Evangelization") of [the Gospel](/wiki/The_Gospel "The Gospel") as well as [Catholic social teaching](/wiki/Catholic_social_teaching "Catholic social teaching"), which emphasizes voluntary support for the sick, the poor, and the afflicted through the [corporal and spiritual works of mercy](/wiki/Works_of_mercy "Works of mercy"). The Catholic Church operates thousands of [Catholic schools](/wiki/Catholic_schools "Catholic schools"), [universities](/wiki/Catholic_higher_education "Catholic higher education"), [hospitals](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_health_care "Catholic Church and health care"), and orphanages around the world, and is the largest non-government provider of [education](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school") and [health care](/wiki/Healthcare_and_the_Catholic_Church "Healthcare and the Catholic Church") in the world.[[388]](#cite_note-398) Among its other social services are numerous charitable and humanitarian organizations.

[Canon law](/wiki/Canon_law_of_the_Catholic_Church "Canon law of the Catholic Church") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *jus canonicum*)[[389]](#cite_note-399) is the [system](/wiki/Legal_system "Legal system") of [laws](/wiki/Law "Law") and [legal principles](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") made and enforced by the [hierarchical authorities](/wiki/Hierarchy_of_the_Catholic_Church "Hierarchy of the Catholic Church") of the Catholic Church to regulate its external organisation and government and to order and direct the activities of Catholics toward the mission of the church.[[390]](#cite_note-400) The canon law of the Latin Church was the first modern Western [legal system](/wiki/Legal_system "Legal system"),[[391]](#cite_note-401) and is the oldest continuously functioning legal system in the West.[[392]](#cite_note-402)[[393]](#cite_note-403) while the distinctive traditions of [Eastern Catholic canon law](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_canon_law "Eastern Catholic canon law") govern the 23 Eastern Catholic [particular churches](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church") *[sui iuris](/wiki/Sui_iuris "Sui iuris").*

As the world's oldest and largest continuously functioning international institution,[[394]](#cite_note-404) it has played a prominent role in the history and development of [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture").[[395]](#cite_note-405)[[396]](#cite_note-406)[[397]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBokenkotter2004Preface-407)[[398]](#cite_note-408) The 2,834 [sees](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see")[[399]](#cite_note-409) are grouped into [24 particular autonomous Churches](/wiki/Sui_iuris#Catholic_ecclesiastical_use "Sui iuris") (the largest of which being the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church")), each with its own distinct traditions regarding the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") and the administering of [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church").[[400]](#cite_note-410) With more than 1.1 billion baptized members, the Catholic Church is the largest Christian [church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") and represents 50.1%[[1]](#cite_note-Global_Christianity-1) of all Christians as well as 16.7% of the [world's population](/wiki/World_population "World population").[[401]](#cite_note-411)[[402]](#cite_note-Adherents-412)[[403]](#cite_note-413) Catholics live all over the world through [missions](/wiki/Catholic_missions "Catholic missions"), [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), and [conversions](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity").

### Eastern Orthodox Church

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Church_of_St._George%2C_Istanbul_%28August_2010%29.jpg/250px-Church_of_St._George%2C_Istanbul_%28August_2010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_of_St._George,_Istanbul_(August_2010).jpg)

[St. George's Cathedral](/wiki/St._George%27s_Cathedral,_Istanbul "St. George's Cathedral, Istanbul") in [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"): It has been the seat of the [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") whose leader is regarded as the *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").[[404]](#cite_note-414)

The Eastern Orthodox Church consists of those churches in communion with the [patriarchal](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch") sees of the East, such as the [Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople").[[405]](#cite_note-415) Like the Catholic Church, the Eastern Orthodox Church also traces its heritage to the foundation of Christianity through [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") and has an [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_polity "Episcopal polity") structure, though the [autonomy](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") of its component parts is emphasized, and most of them are national churches.

[Eastern Orthodox theology](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_theology "Eastern Orthodox theology") is based on [holy tradition](/wiki/Holy_tradition "Holy tradition") which incorporates the dogmatic decrees of the [seven Ecumenical Councils](/wiki/First_seven_ecumenical_councils "First seven ecumenical councils"), the Scriptures, and the teaching of the [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"). The church teaches that it is the [one, holy, catholic and apostolic](/wiki/Four_Marks_of_the_Church "Four Marks of the Church") [church](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church") established by [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") in his [Great Commission](/wiki/Great_Commission "Great Commission"),[[406]](#cite_note-416) and that its bishops are the successors of Christ's apostles.[[407]](#cite_note-417) It maintains that it practises the original Christian faith, as passed down by holy tradition. Its [patriarchates](/wiki/Patriarchates "Patriarchates"), reminiscent of the [pentarchy](/wiki/Pentarchy "Pentarchy"), and other [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") and [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomous "Autonomous") churches reflect a variety of [hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchical "Hierarchical") [organisation](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church_organization "Eastern Orthodox Church organization"). It recognizes seven major sacraments, of which the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") is the principal one, celebrated [liturgically](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") in [synaxis](/wiki/Synaxis "Synaxis"). The church teaches that through [consecration](/wiki/Consecration#Eucharist "Consecration") [invoked](/wiki/Epiclesis "Epiclesis") by a [priest](/wiki/Priesthood_(Orthodox_Church) "Priesthood (Orthodox Church)"), the sacrificial bread and wine become the body and blood of Christ. The [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") is [venerated](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") in the Eastern Orthodox Church as the *[Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos")*, meaning God-bearer, and is honoured in [devotions](/wiki/Marian_devotions#Eastern_Orthodoxy "Marian devotions").

Eastern Orthodoxy is the second largest single denomination in Christianity, with an estimated 300 million adherents,[[408]](#cite_note-:4-418) although [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") collectively outnumber them, substantially.[[1]](#cite_note-Global_Christianity-1)[[409]](#cite_note-CSGC2019-419) As one of the oldest surviving religious institutions in the world, the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") has played a prominent role in the history and culture of [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeastern_Europe "Southeastern Europe"), the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), and the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East").[[410]](#cite_note-420) The majority of Eastern Orthodox Christians live mainly in [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"), and parts of the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") region, [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), and the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East"). Over half of Eastern Orthodox Christians follow the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), while the vast majority live within [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[411]](#cite_note-421) There are also communities in the former [Byzantine regions](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") of Africa, the [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean"), and in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"). Eastern Orthodox communities are also present in many other parts of the world, particularly North America, Western Europe, and Australia, formed through [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora"), [conversions](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity"), and [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") activity.[[412]](#cite_note-422)

### Oriental Orthodox Churches

Main article: [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Addis_abeba%2C_chiesa_della_trinit%C3%A0%2C_esterno_02.jpg/250px-Addis_abeba%2C_chiesa_della_trinit%C3%A0%2C_esterno_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Addis_abeba,_chiesa_della_trinit%C3%A0,_esterno_02.jpg)

[Holy Trinity Cathedral](/wiki/Holy_Trinity_Cathedral_(Addis_Ababa) "Holy Trinity Cathedral (Addis Ababa)") in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), the seat of the Ethiopian Orthodox; the largest of the Oriental Orthodox Churches

The [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") (also called "Old Oriental" churches) are those eastern churches that recognize the first three ecumenical councils—[Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), [Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople"), and [Ephesus](/wiki/First_Council_of_Ephesus "First Council of Ephesus")—but reject the dogmatic definitions of the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") and instead espouse a [Miaphysite christology](/wiki/Miaphysite_christology "Miaphysite christology").

The Oriental Orthodox communion consists of six groups: [Syriac Orthodox](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox "Syriac Orthodox"), [Coptic Orthodox](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), [Ethiopian Orthodox](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox "Ethiopian Orthodox"), [Eritrean Orthodox](/wiki/Eritrean_Orthodox "Eritrean Orthodox"), [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church") (India), and [Armenian Apostolic](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic "Armenian Apostolic") churches.[[413]](#cite_note-423) These six churches, while being in communion with each other, are completely independent hierarchically.[[414]](#cite_note-424) These churches are generally not in communion with the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), with whom they are in dialogue for erecting a communion.[[415]](#cite_note-sor.cua.edu-425) Together, they have about 62 million members worldwide.[[416]](#cite_note-426)[[417]](#cite_note-427)[[418]](#cite_note-428)[[419]](#cite_note-429)[[409]](#cite_note-CSGC2019-419)

As some of the oldest religious institutions in the world, the Oriental Orthodox Churches have played a prominent role in the history and culture of [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [Sudan](/wiki/Christian_Nubia "Christian Nubia"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"),[[420]](#cite_note-430) and parts of the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [India](/wiki/India "India").[[421]](#cite_note-431)[[422]](#cite_note-432)[[423]](#cite_note-433) An Eastern Christian body of [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") [churches](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), its [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") are equal by virtue of [episcopal ordination](/wiki/Episcopal_ordination "Episcopal ordination"), and its doctrines can be summarized in that the churches recognize the validity of only the first three [ecumenical councils](/wiki/Ecumenical_council "Ecumenical council").[[424]](#cite_note-434)

Some followers of Oriental Orthodox Churches follow cultural practices that are reminiscent of [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") practices, like [abstaining from pork](/wiki/Religious_restrictions_on_the_consumption_of_pork "Religious restrictions on the consumption of pork"),[[425]](#cite_note-435) [circumcising](/wiki/Circumcising "Circumcising") male babies,[[426]](#cite_note-436) and observing [ritual purification](/wiki/Ritual_purification "Ritual purification").[[427]](#cite_note-437)[[428]](#cite_note-438)

### Church of the East

Further information: [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg/250px-Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_of_Saint_John_the_Arab.jpg)

A 6th-century [Nestorian church](/wiki/Nestorian_church "Nestorian church"), St. John the Arab, in the [Assyrian village](/wiki/Assyrian_tribes "Assyrian tribes") of [Geramon](/wiki/Andac "Andac") in [Hakkari](/wiki/Hakkari_(historical_region) "Hakkari (historical region)"), southeastern Turkey.

The [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East"), which was part of the [Great Church](/wiki/Great_Church "Great Church"),[[429]](#cite_note-439) shared [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") with those in the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") until the [Council of Ephesus](/wiki/Council_of_Ephesus "Council of Ephesus") [condemned Nestorius](/wiki/Nestorian_schism "Nestorian schism") in 431. Continuing as a *[dhimmi](/wiki/Dhimmi "Dhimmi")* community under the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") after the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia") (633–654), the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") played a major role in the history of [Christianity in Asia](/wiki/Christianity_in_Asia "Christianity in Asia"). Between the 9th and 14th centuries, it represented the world's largest [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") in terms of geographical extent. It established [dioceses](/wiki/Dioceses_of_the_Church_of_the_East_to_1318 "Dioceses of the Church of the East to 1318") and communities stretching from the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and today's Iraq and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), to [India](/wiki/India_(East_Syriac_ecclesiastical_province) "India (East Syriac ecclesiastical province)") (the [Saint Thomas Syrian Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") of [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala")), the [Mongol kingdoms](/wiki/Christianity_among_the_Mongols "Christianity among the Mongols") in Central Asia, and [China](/wiki/Church_of_the_East_in_China "Church of the East in China") during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (7th–9th centuries). In the 13th and 14th centuries, the church experienced a final period of expansion under the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire"), where influential Church of the East clergy sat in the Mongol court.

The [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), with an unbroken patriarchate established in the 17th century, is an independent [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christian "Eastern Christian") denomination which claims continuity from the [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East")—in parallel to the Catholic patriarchate established in the 16th century that evolved into the [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), an [Eastern Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic "Eastern Catholic") church in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"). It is an Eastern Christian [church](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") that follows the traditional [christology](/wiki/Christology "Christology") and [ecclesiology](/wiki/Ecclesiology "Ecclesiology") of the historical Church of the East. Largely [aniconic](/wiki/Aniconism_in_Christianity "Aniconism in Christianity") and not in [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christianity) "Communion (Christianity)") with any other church, it belongs to the eastern branch of [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"), and uses the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") in its [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy").[[430]](#cite_note-440)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Church_of_Saint_Mary_-_Urmia_-_Iran_-_%DA%A9%D9%84%DB%8C%D8%B3%D8%A7%DB%8C_%D9%86%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D8%B1%DB%8C%D9%85%D8%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%85%DB%8C%D9%87_-_%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86.jpg/250px-Church_of_Saint_Mary_-_Urmia_-_Iran_-_%DA%A9%D9%84%DB%8C%D8%B3%D8%A7%DB%8C_%D9%86%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D8%B1%DB%8C%D9%85%D8%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%85%DB%8C%D9%87_-_%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_of_Saint_Mary_-_Urmia_-_Iran_-_%DA%A9%D9%84%DB%8C%D8%B3%D8%A7%DB%8C_%D9%86%D9%86%D9%87_%D9%85%D8%B1%DB%8C%D9%85%D8%8C_%D8%A7%D8%B1%D9%88%D9%85%DB%8C%D9%87_-_%D8%A7%DB%8C%D8%B1%D8%A7%D9%86.jpg)

[Saint Mary Church](/wiki/St._Mary_Church,_Urmia "St. Mary Church, Urmia"); an ancient Assyrian church located in the city of [Urmia](/wiki/Urmia "Urmia"), Iran

Its main spoken language is [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"), a dialect of [Eastern Aramaic](/wiki/Eastern_Aramaic "Eastern Aramaic"), and the majority of its adherents are ethnic [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people"), mostly living in [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), [India](/wiki/India "India") ([Chaldean Syrian Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Syrian_Church "Chaldean Syrian Church")), and in the [Assyrian diaspora](/wiki/Assyrian_diaspora "Assyrian diaspora").[[431]](#cite_note-441) It is officially headquartered in the city of [Erbil](/wiki/Erbil "Erbil") in northern [Iraqi Kurdistan](/wiki/Iraqi_Kurdistan "Iraqi Kurdistan"), and its original area also spreads into south-eastern Turkey and north-western Iran, corresponding to ancient [Assyria](/wiki/Assyria "Assyria"). Its hierarchy is composed of [metropolitan bishops](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") and [diocesan bishops](/wiki/Diocesan_bishop "Diocesan bishop"), while lower clergy consists of [priests](/wiki/Priest "Priest") and [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), who serve in dioceses (eparchies) and parishes throughout the Middle East, India, North America, Oceania, and Europe (including the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") and Russia).[[432]](#cite_note-442)

The [Ancient Church of the East](/wiki/Ancient_Church_of_the_East "Ancient Church of the East") distinguished itself from the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") in 1964. It is one of the [Assyrian](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") churches that claim continuity with the historical Church of the East, one of the oldest Christian churches in Mesopotamia.[[433]](#cite_note-443) It is officially headquartered in the city of [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), Iraq.[[434]](#cite_note-blackwell1-444) The majority of its adherents are ethnic [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people").[[434]](#cite_note-blackwell1-444)

### Protestantism

Main articles: [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") and [Proto-Protestantism](/wiki/Proto-Protestantism "Proto-Protestantism")

See also: [Protestant ecclesiology](/wiki/Protestant_ecclesiology "Protestant ecclesiology")

In 1521, the [Edict of Worms](/wiki/Edict_of_Worms "Edict of Worms") condemned [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") and officially banned citizens of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") from defending or propagating his ideas.[[435]](#cite_note-445) This split within the Roman Catholic church is now called the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). Prominent Reformers included Martin Luther, [Huldrych Zwingli](/wiki/Huldrych_Zwingli "Huldrych Zwingli"), and [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin"). The 1529 [Protestation at Speyer](/wiki/Protestation_at_Speyer "Protestation at Speyer") against being excommunicated gave this party the name [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"). Luther's primary theological heirs are known as [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"). Zwingli and Calvin's heirs are far broader denominationally and are referred to as the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism").[[436]](#cite_note-446) The [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") churches descended from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and organized in the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"). Some Lutherans identify as [Evangelical Catholics](/wiki/Evangelical_Catholic "Evangelical Catholic") and some but not all Anglicans consider themselves both Protestant and Catholic.[[437]](#cite_note-447)[[438]](#cite_note-448)[[439]](#cite_note-449) Protestants have developed [their own culture](/wiki/Protestant_culture "Protestant culture"), with major contributions in education, the [humanities and sciences](/wiki/Merton_thesis "Merton thesis"), the political and social order, the [economy](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic") and the arts, and many other fields.[[440]](#cite_note-450)[[441]](#cite_note-451)

Since the Anglican, Lutheran, and the Reformed branches of Protestantism originated for the most part in cooperation with the government, these movements are termed the "[Magisterial Reformation](/wiki/Magisterial_Reformation "Magisterial Reformation")". On the other hand, groups such as the [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism"), who often do not consider themselves to be Protestant, originated in the [Radical Reformation](/wiki/Radical_Reformation "Radical Reformation"), which though sometimes protected under *Acts of Toleration*, do not trace their history back to any state church. They are further distinguished by their rejection of infant baptism; they believe in baptism only of adult believers—[credobaptism](/wiki/Credobaptism "Credobaptism") (Anabaptists include the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Apostolic](/wiki/Apostolic_Christian_Church "Apostolic Christian Church"), [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof "Bruderhof"), [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites"), [River Brethren](/wiki/River_Brethren "River Brethren") and [Schwarzenau Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren") groups.)[[442]](#cite_note-452)[[443]](#cite_note-453)[[444]](#cite_note-454)[[445]](#cite_note-455)

The term *Protestant* also refers to any churches which formed later, with either the Magisterial or Radical traditions. In the 18th century, for example, [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") grew out of [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") minister [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley")'s [evangelical revival movement](/wiki/Evangelical_Revival "Evangelical Revival").[[446]](#cite_note-456) Several [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") and [non-denominational churches](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity"), which emphasize the cleansing power of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity"), in turn grew out of Methodism.[[447]](#cite_note-457) Because Methodists, Pentecostals and other evangelicals stress "accepting Jesus as your personal Lord and Savior",[[448]](#cite_note-458) which comes from Wesley's emphasis of the [New Birth](/wiki/Born_again_(Christianity) "Born again (Christianity)"),[[449]](#cite_note-459) they often refer to themselves as being [born again](/wiki/Born_again "Born again").[[450]](#cite_note-460)[[451]](#cite_note-461)

Protestantism is the second largest major group of Christians after Catholicism by number of followers, although the Eastern Orthodox Church is larger than any single Protestant denomination.[[402]](#cite_note-Adherents-412) Estimates vary, mainly over the question of which denominations to classify as Protestant. The total Protestant population has reached 1.17 billion in 2024, corresponding to nearly 44% of the world's Christians.[[306]](#cite_note-gordonconwell.edu-315)[[409]](#cite_note-CSGC2019-419)[[303]](#cite_note-pewforum1-312)[[307]](#cite_note-KimOsmerSchweitzer2018-316)[[452]](#cite_note-462) The majority of Protestants are members of just a handful of denominational families, i.e. [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), [Anabaptism](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism") ([Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), [Apostolic](/wiki/Apostolic_Christian_Church "Apostolic Christian Church"), [Bruderhof](/wiki/Bruderhof_Communities "Bruderhof Communities"), [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites"), [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), [River Brethren](/wiki/River_Brethren "River Brethren"), and [Schwarzenau Brethren](/wiki/Schwarzenau_Brethren "Schwarzenau Brethren")), [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), [Moravianism/Hussites](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"), [Plymouth Brethren](/wiki/Plymouth_Brethren "Plymouth Brethren"), [Quakerism](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [Reformed Christianity](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") ([Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_Protestantism "Continental Reformed Protestantism"), [Reformed Anglicans](/wiki/Reformed_Anglican "Reformed Anglican"), and [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism")),[[453]](#cite_note-463) and [Waldensianism](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") are the main families of Protestantism.[[8]](#cite_note-Melton2005-10)[[303]](#cite_note-pewforum1-312) [Nondenominational](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity"), [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), [charismatic](/wiki/Charismatic_Movement "Charismatic Movement"), [neo-charismatic](/wiki/Neo-charismatic_churches "Neo-charismatic churches"), independent, and other churches are on the rise, and constitute a significant part of Protestant Christianity.[[454]](#cite_note-464)

Some groups of individuals who hold basic Protestant tenets identify themselves as "Christians" or "[born-again](/wiki/Born-again "Born-again") Christians". They typically distance themselves from the [confessionalism](/wiki/Confessionalism_(religion) "Confessionalism (religion)") and [creedalism](/wiki/Creed "Creed") of other Christian communities[[455]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-465) by calling themselves "[non-denominational](/wiki/Non-denominational_Christianity "Non-denominational Christianity")" or "[evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical")". Often founded by individual pastors, they have little affiliation with historic denominations.[[456]](#cite_note-466)

Historical chart of the main Protestant branches

[![Historical chart of the main Protestant branches](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Protestant_branches.svg/960px-Protestant_branches.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Protestant_branches.svg "Historical chart of the main Protestant branches")

### Restorationism

Main article: [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Priesthood03080u.jpg/250px-Priesthood03080u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Priesthood03080u.jpg)

A 19th-century drawing of [Joseph Smith](/wiki/Joseph_Smith "Joseph Smith") and [Oliver Cowdery](/wiki/Oliver_Cowdery "Oliver Cowdery") receiving the [Aaronic priesthood](/wiki/Aaronic_priesthood_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Aaronic priesthood (Latter Day Saints)") from [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"). [Latter Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") believe that the [Priesthood](/wiki/Christian_priest "Christian priest") ceased to exist after the death of the apostles and therefore needed to be [restored](/wiki/Restoration_(Latter_Day_Saints) "Restoration (Latter Day Saints)").

The [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening"), a period of religious revival that occurred in the United States during the early 1800s, saw the development of a number of unrelated churches. They generally saw themselves as [restoring](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism") the original church of Jesus Christ rather than reforming one of the existing churches.[[457]](#cite_note-467) A common belief held by Restorationists was that the other divisions of Christianity had introduced doctrinal defects into Christianity, which was known as the [Great Apostasy](/wiki/Great_Apostasy "Great Apostasy").[[458]](#cite_note-468) In Asia, [Iglesia ni Cristo](/wiki/Iglesia_ni_Cristo "Iglesia ni Cristo") is a known Restorationist denomination that was established during the early 1900s. Other examples of Restorationist denominations include [Irvingianism](/wiki/Catholic_Apostolic_Church "Catholic Apostolic Church") and [Swedenborgianism](/wiki/Swedenborgians "Swedenborgians").[[459]](#cite_note-469)[[10]](#cite_note-Spinks2017-13)

Some of the churches originating during this period are historically connected to early 19th-century camp meetings in the Midwest and upstate New York. One of the largest churches produced from the movement is [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints").[[460]](#cite_note-470) American [Millennialism](/wiki/Millennialism "Millennialism") and [Adventism](/wiki/Adventism "Adventism"), which arose from Evangelical Protestantism, influenced the [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") movement and, as a reaction specifically to [William Miller](/wiki/William_Miller_(preacher) "William Miller (preacher)"), the [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"). Others, including the [Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)](/wiki/Christian_Church_(Disciples_of_Christ) "Christian Church (Disciples of Christ)"), [Evangelical Christian Church in Canada](/wiki/Evangelical_Christian_Church_in_Canada "Evangelical Christian Church in Canada"),[[461]](#cite_note-471)[[462]](#cite_note-472) [Churches of Christ](/wiki/Churches_of_Christ "Churches of Christ"), and the [Christian churches and churches of Christ](/wiki/Christian_churches_and_churches_of_Christ "Christian churches and churches of Christ"), have their roots in the contemporaneous Stone-Campbell [Restoration Movement](/wiki/Restoration_Movement "Restoration Movement"), which was centered in Kentucky and Tennessee. Other groups originating in this time period include the [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians") and the previously mentioned [Latter Day Saints movement](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saints_movement "Latter Day Saints movement"). While the churches originating in the Second Great Awakening have some superficial similarities, their doctrine and practices vary significantly.[[463]](#cite_note-473)

### Other

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Biserica_unitarian%C4%83_20180321_115158_05.jpg/250px-Biserica_unitarian%C4%83_20180321_115158_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biserica_unitarian%C4%83_20180321_115158_05.jpg)

[Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") in [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca")

Within Italy, Poland, Lithuania, Transylvania, Hungary, Romania, and the United Kingdom, [Unitarian Churches](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") emerged from the [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") tradition in the 16th century;[[464]](#cite_note-474) the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") is an example of such a denomination that arose in this era.[[465]](#cite_note-475) They adopted the [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") doctrine of [credobaptism](/wiki/Credobaptism "Credobaptism").[[466]](#cite_note-476)

Various smaller [Independent Catholic](/wiki/Independent_Catholic "Independent Catholic") communities, such as the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"),[[467]](#cite_note-477) include the word *[Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_(term) "Catholic (term)")* in their title, and arguably have more or less liturgical practices in common with the Catholic Church but are no longer in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See").[[468]](#cite_note-478)

[Spiritual Christians](/wiki/Spiritual_Christians "Spiritual Christians"), such as the [Doukhobors](/wiki/Doukhobors "Doukhobors") and [Molokans](/wiki/Molokan "Molokan"), broke from the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") and maintain close association with Mennonites and [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") due to similar religious practices; all of these groups are furthermore collectively considered to be [peace churches](/wiki/Peace_churches "Peace churches") due to their belief in [pacifism](/wiki/Christian_pacifism "Christian pacifism").[[469]](#cite_note-479)[[470]](#cite_note-480)

[Messianic Judaism](/wiki/Messianic_Judaism "Messianic Judaism") (or the Messianic Movement) is the name of a Christian movement comprising a number of streams, whose members may consider themselves Jewish. The movement originated in the 1960s and 1970s, and it blends elements of religious Jewish practice with evangelical Christianity. Messianic Judaism affirms Christian creeds such as the messiahship and divinity of "Yeshua" (the Hebrew name of Jesus) and the Triune Nature of God, while also adhering to some Jewish dietary laws and customs.[[471]](#cite_note-481)

[Esoteric Christians](/wiki/Esoteric_Christianity "Esoteric Christianity"), such as [The Christian Community](/wiki/The_Christian_Community "The Christian Community"),[[472]](#cite_note-482) regard Christianity as a [mystery religion](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism")[[473]](#cite_note-483)[[474]](#cite_note-484) and profess the existence and possession of certain [esoteric](/wiki/Western_esotericism "Western esotericism") doctrines or practices,[[475]](#cite_note-485)[[476]](#cite_note-486) hidden from the public and accessible only to a narrow circle of "enlightened", "initiated", or highly educated people.[[477]](#cite_note-487)[[478]](#cite_note-488)

[Nondenominational Christianity](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") or non-denominational Christianity consists of [churches](/wiki/Simple_church "Simple church") which typically distance themselves from the [confessionalism](/wiki/Confessionalism_(religion) "Confessionalism (religion)") or [creedalism](/wiki/Creed "Creed") of other Christian communities[[455]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-465) by not formally aligning with a specific [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination").[[479]](#cite_note-Hughes1997-489) Nondenominational Christianity first arose in the 18th century through the [Stone-Campbell Restoration Movement](/wiki/Stone-Campbell_Restoration_Movement "Stone-Campbell Restoration Movement"), with followers organizing themselves as "[Christians](/wiki/Christians_(Stone_Movement) "Christians (Stone Movement)")" and "[Disciples of Christ](/wiki/Disciples_of_Christ_(Campbell_Movement) "Disciples of Christ (Campbell Movement)")",[[note 11]](#cite_note-491)[[479]](#cite_note-Hughes1997-489)[[480]](#cite_note-Barnett2020-490) but many typically adhere to [evangelical Christianity](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity").[[481]](#cite_note-492)[[482]](#cite_note-493)[[483]](#cite_note-494)

## Cultural influence

Main articles: [Christian culture](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture") and [Role of Christianity in civilization](/wiki/Role_of_Christianity_in_civilization "Role of Christianity in civilization")

Further information: [Protestant culture](/wiki/Protestant_culture "Protestant culture") and [Christian influences in Islam](/wiki/Christian_influences_in_Islam "Christian influences in Islam")

[Christian culture](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/La_volta_della_Cappella_Sistina_%28Michelangelo_Buonarroti_1508-1512%29_-_panoramio.jpg/330px-La_volta_della_Cappella_Sistina_%28Michelangelo_Buonarroti_1508-1512%29_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_volta_della_Cappella_Sistina_(Michelangelo_Buonarroti_1508-1512)_-_panoramio.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Hollfeld_Krippe_P1340403.jpg/250px-Hollfeld_Krippe_P1340403.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollfeld_Krippe_P1340403.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/NotreDameDeParis.jpg/120px-NotreDameDeParis.jpg)](/wiki/File:NotreDameDeParis.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Christ_the_Redeemer_-_Cristo_Redentor.jpg/120px-Christ_the_Redeemer_-_Cristo_Redentor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_the_Redeemer_-_Cristo_Redentor.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Svatba_%282%29.jpg/250px-Svatba_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Svatba_(2).jpg)

*Clockwise from top*: [Sistine Chapel ceiling](/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling "Sistine Chapel ceiling"), [Notre-Dame](/wiki/Notre-Dame_de_Paris "Notre-Dame de Paris") cathedral in Paris, Eastern Orthodox [wedding](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding"), *[Christ the Redeemer](/wiki/Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue) "Christ the Redeemer (statue)")* statue, [Nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene")

The history of the [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") spans about 1,700 years and includes a variety of socio-political developments, as well as advances in the [arts](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_cathedrals_and_great_churches "Architecture of cathedrals and great churches"), [literature](/wiki/Christian_literature "Christian literature"), [science](/wiki/Christianity_and_science "Christianity and science"), [philosophy](/wiki/Christian_philosophy "Christian philosophy"), and technology.[[484]](#cite_note-Crisis_in_Western_Education-495)[[485]](#cite_note-496)[[486]](#cite_note-497) Since the spread of Christianity from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") to [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") during the early [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), Christendom has been divided in the pre-existing [Greek East and Latin West](/wiki/Greek_East_and_Latin_West "Greek East and Latin West"). Consequently, different versions of the Christian cultures arose with their own rites and practices, centered around the cities of Rome ([Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity")) and [Carthage](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Carthage "Archdiocese of Carthage"), whose communities were called Western or Latin Christendom,[[487]](#cite_note-498) and [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") ([Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity")), [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch") ([Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity")), [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala") ([Indian Christianity](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians")) and [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") ([Coptic Christianity](/wiki/Coptic_Christianity "Coptic Christianity")), whose communities were called Eastern or Oriental Christendom.[[488]](#cite_note-499)[[489]](#cite_note-500)[[490]](#cite_note-501) The [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") was one of the peaks in [Christian history](/wiki/Christian_history "Christian history") and Eastern [Christian civilization](/wiki/Christian_civilization "Christian civilization").[[46]](#cite_note-Cameron_2006_42-51) From the 11th to 13th centuries, [Latin Christendom](/wiki/Latin_Christendom "Latin Christendom") rose to the central role of the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").

The Bible has had a profound influence on Western civilization and on cultures around the globe; it has contributed to the formation of [Western law](/wiki/Western_law "Western law"), [art](/wiki/Western_art "Western art"), [texts](/wiki/Western_literature "Western literature"), and education.[[491]](#cite_note-502)[[492]](#cite_note-503)[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiches2000ch._1-504) With a literary tradition spanning two millennia, the Bible is one of the most influential works ever written. From practices of [personal hygiene](/wiki/Hygiene_in_Christianity "Hygiene in Christianity") to philosophy and ethics, the Bible has directly and indirectly influenced politics and law, war and peace, sexual morals, marriage and family life, toilet etiquette, letters and learning, the arts, economics, social justice, medical care and more.[[493]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiches2000ch._1-504)

[Christians](/wiki/Lists_of_Christians "Lists of Christians") have made a myriad of contributions to [human progress](/wiki/Progress_(history) "Progress (history)") in a broad and diverse range of fields, including philosophy,[[494]](#cite_note-505) [science and technology](/wiki/List_of_Christians_in_science_and_technology "List of Christians in science and technology"),[[495]](#cite_note-506)[[496]](#cite_note-507)[[497]](#cite_note-508)[[498]](#cite_note-509) [medicine](/wiki/Catholic_Church_and_health_care "Catholic Church and health care"),[[499]](#cite_note-510) [fine arts and architecture](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_Church_artists "List of Catholic Church artists"),[[500]](#cite_note-511) [politics](/wiki/Christianity_and_politics "Christianity and politics"), [literatures](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_authors "List of Catholic authors"), [music](/wiki/Christian_music "Christian music"),[[501]](#cite_note-512) and business.[[502]](#cite_note-513)[[503]](#cite_note-514) According to *100 Years of Nobel Prizes* a review of the Nobel Prizes award between 1901 and 2000 reveals that (65.4%) of [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prizes "Nobel Prizes") Laureates, [have identified Christianity](/wiki/List_of_Christian_Nobel_laureates "List of Christian Nobel laureates") in its various forms as their religious preference.[[504]](#cite_note-515)

Outside the Western world, Christianity has had an influence on various cultures, such as in Africa, the Near East, Middle East, East Asia, Southeast Asia, and the Indian subcontinent.[[505]](#cite_note-Curtis_2017_173-516)[[506]](#cite_note-517) [Eastern Christian scientists and scholars of the medieval Islamic world](/wiki/List_of_Christian_scientists_and_scholars_of_the_medieval_Islamic_world "List of Christian scientists and scholars of the medieval Islamic world") (particularly [Jacobite](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") and [Nestorian](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism") Christians) contributed to the [Islamic civilization](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") during the reign of the [Ummayyads](/wiki/Ummayyad "Ummayyad") and the [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid"), by translating works of [Greek philosophers](/wiki/Greek_philosophers "Greek philosophers") to [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") and afterwards, to [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language").[[507]](#cite_note-518)[[508]](#cite_note-519)[[509]](#cite_note-520) They also excelled in philosophy, science, theology, and medicine.[[510]](#cite_note-521)[[511]](#cite_note-522)[[512]](#cite_note-523) Scholars and intellectuals agree [Christians in the Middle East](/wiki/Christians_in_the_Middle_East "Christians in the Middle East") have made significant contributions to Islamic civilization since the introduction of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), and they have had a significant impact contributing the culture of the [Mashriq](/wiki/Mashriq "Mashriq"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran").[[513]](#cite_note-524)[[505]](#cite_note-Curtis_2017_173-516)

### Influence on Western culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/St-thomas-aquinasFXD.jpg/250px-St-thomas-aquinasFXD.jpg)](/wiki/File:St-thomas-aquinasFXD.jpg)

[Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas"), an Italian [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") [friar](/wiki/Friar "Friar") and [priest](/wiki/Catholic_priest "Catholic priest"), the foremost [Scholastic](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") thinker,[[514]](#cite_note-525) as well as one of the most influential philosophers and theologians in the Western tradition.[[515]](#cite_note-526)

[Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"), throughout most of its history, has been nearly equivalent to [Christian culture](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture"), and a large portion of the population of the Western Hemisphere can be described as practicing or nominal Christians. The notion of "Europe" and the "Western World" has been intimately connected with the concept of "Christianity and Christendom". Many historians even attribute Christianity for being the link that created a unified [European identity](/wiki/European_identity "European identity").[[516]](#cite_note-527)

Though Western culture contained several polytheistic religions during its early years under the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Roman Empires](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), as the centralized Roman power waned, the dominance of the Catholic Church was the only consistent force in Western Europe.[[517]](#cite_note-Koch-1994-528) Until the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"),[[517]](#cite_note-Koch-1994-528) Christian culture guided the course of philosophy, literature, art, music and science.[[517]](#cite_note-Koch-1994-528)[[484]](#cite_note-Crisis_in_Western_Education-495) Christian disciplines of the respective arts have subsequently developed into [Christian philosophy](/wiki/Christian_philosophy "Christian philosophy"), [Christian art](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art"), [Christian music](/wiki/Christian_music "Christian music"), [Christian literature](/wiki/Christian_literature "Christian literature"), and so on.

Christianity has had a significant impact on education, as the church created the bases of the Western system of education,[[518]](#cite_note-Britannica2022-529) and was the sponsor of [founding universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") in the Western world, as the university is generally regarded as an institution that has its origin in the [Medieval Christian](/wiki/History_of_Christianity "History of Christianity") setting.[[66]](#cite_note-verger1999-71) Historically, Christianity has often been a patron of science and medicine; many [Catholic clergy](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_cleric-scientists "List of Catholic cleric-scientists"),[[519]](#cite_note-530) [Jesuits](/wiki/List_of_Jesuit_scientists "List of Jesuit scientists") in particular,[[520]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoods2005109-531)[[521]](#cite_note-532)[[522]](#cite_note-533) have been active in the sciences throughout history and have made significant contributions to the [development of science](/wiki/Christianity_and_science "Christianity and science").[[523]](#cite_note-534)[[524]](#cite_note-535) Some scholars state that Christianity contributed to the rise of the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution").[[525]](#cite_note-536) Protestantism also has had an important influence on science. According to the [Merton Thesis](/wiki/Merton_Thesis "Merton Thesis"), there was a positive correlation between the rise of English [Puritanism](/wiki/Puritanism "Puritanism") and German [Pietism](/wiki/Pietism "Pietism") on the one hand, and early experimental science on the other.[[526]](#cite_note-537)[[527]](#cite_note-538)[[528]](#cite_note-539)
The civilizing influence of Christianity includes social welfare,[[518]](#cite_note-Britannica2022-529) contribution to the medical and health care,[[529]](#cite_note-540) founding hospitals,[[518]](#cite_note-Britannica2022-529) economics (as the [Protestant work ethic](/wiki/Protestant_work_ethic "Protestant work ethic")),[[518]](#cite_note-Britannica2022-529)[[530]](#cite_note-541)[[531]](#cite_note-542) architecture,[[532]](#cite_note-543) literature,[[533]](#cite_note-544) [personal hygiene](/wiki/Hygiene_in_Christianity "Hygiene in Christianity") ([ablution](/wiki/Ablution_in_Christianity "Ablution in Christianity")),[[534]](#cite_note-545)[[535]](#cite_note-546) and family life.[[518]](#cite_note-Britannica2022-529)[[536]](#cite_note-547) Historically, *[extended families](/wiki/Extended_family "Extended family")* were the basic family unit in the [Christian culture](/wiki/Christian_culture "Christian culture") and [countries](/wiki/Christian_countries "Christian countries").[[537]](#cite_note-548)

[Cultural Christians](/wiki/Cultural_Christian "Cultural Christian") are secular people with a Christian heritage who may not believe in the religious claims of Christianity, but who retain an affinity for the popular culture, art, [music](/wiki/Christian_music "Christian music"), and so on related to the religion.[[538]](#cite_note-549)

*[Postchristianity](/wiki/Postchristianity "Postchristianity")* is a term for the [decline of Christianity](/wiki/Decline_of_Christianity "Decline of Christianity"), particularly in [Europe](/wiki/Christianity_in_Europe "Christianity in Europe"), [Canada](/wiki/Religion_in_Canada "Religion in Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Christianity_in_Australia "Christianity in Australia"), and to a minor degree the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone"), in the 20th and 21st centuries, considered in terms of [postmodernism](/wiki/Postmodernism "Postmodernism"). It refers to the loss of Christianity's monopoly on [values](/wiki/Values "Values") and [world view](/wiki/World_view "World view") in historically Christian societies.[[539]](#cite_note-550)

Christian leaders and congregations have had an influence on the broader [environmental movement](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement"). Christian teachings relate to ecology, and draw on teachings of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") to promote the moral responsibility of humans to care for God's creation. Christianity acknowledges the tension between humanity's duty to care for God's creation and the natural human inclination to resist God's will.[[540]](#cite_note-551) In recent decades, major Christian denominations have revisited their teachings and practices in response to the environmental crisis. There has been a rise in activity in Christian congregations to curb [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"),[[541]](#cite_note-552) partly inspired by the advocacy of [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") following his publication of the encyclical letter [Laudato Si'](/wiki/Laudato_Si%27 "Laudato Si'")—On Care for Our Common Home. Organizations such as Green Christian,[[542]](#cite_note-553) [Laudato Si' Movement](/wiki/Laudato_Si%27_Movement "Laudato Si' Movement"),[[543]](#cite_note-554) and the [Interfaith Center for Sustainable Development](/wiki/Interfaith_Center_for_Sustainable_Development "Interfaith Center for Sustainable Development")[[544]](#cite_note-555) are a few examples of groups working in the Christian and ecology movement. These initiatives reflect how Christians, alongside other people of faith, are increasingly embracing ecological concerns, recognizing that the protection of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") is a spiritual imperative tied to faith and justice.[[545]](#cite_note-556)

## Ecumenism

Main article: [Ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/2bishopsReformation.jpg/250px-2bishopsReformation.jpg)](/wiki/File:2bishopsReformation.jpg)

Bishop [John M. Quinn](/wiki/John_M._Quinn "John M. Quinn") of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Winona](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Winona "Roman Catholic Diocese of Winona") and Bishop Steven Delzer of [Evangelical Lutheran Southeastern Minnesota Synod](/wiki/List_of_ELCA_synods "List of ELCA synods") leading a [Reformation Day](/wiki/Reformation_Day "Reformation Day") service (2017)

Christian groups, sects and [denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations") have long expressed ideals of being reconciled, and in the 20th century, Christian ecumenism advanced in two ways.[[546]](#cite_note-557)[[547]](#cite_note-McManners581-558)[[548]](#cite_note-559) One way was greater cooperation between groups, such as the [World Evangelical Alliance](/wiki/World_Evangelical_Alliance "World Evangelical Alliance") founded in 1846 in London or the [Edinburgh Missionary Conference](/wiki/Edinburgh_Missionary_Conference "Edinburgh Missionary Conference") of Protestants in 1910, the Justice, Peace and Creation Commission of the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches") founded in 1948 by Protestant and Orthodox churches, and similar national councils like the [National Council of Churches in Australia](/wiki/National_Council_of_Churches_in_Australia "National Council of Churches in Australia"), which includes Catholics.[[547]](#cite_note-McManners581-558)

The other way was an institutional union with [united churches](/wiki/United_and_uniting_churches "United and uniting churches"), a practice that can be traced back to unions between Lutherans and Calvinists in early 19th-century Germany. Congregationalist, Methodist, and Presbyterian churches united in 1925 to form the [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada"),[[549]](#cite_note-560) and in 1977 to form the [Uniting Church in Australia](/wiki/Uniting_Church_in_Australia "Uniting Church in Australia"). The [Church of South India](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India") was formed in 1947 by the union of Anglican, Baptist, Methodist, Congregationalist, and Presbyterian churches.[[550]](#cite_note-561)

The [Christian Flag](/wiki/Christian_Flag "Christian Flag") is an ecumenical flag designed in the early 20th century to represent all of Christianity and [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom").[[551]](#cite_note-562) Substantial agreement between various Christian denominations, especially those of Catholicism and Protestantism, has led to a unified presentation of the Christian religion in *[The Common Catechism](/wiki/The_Common_Catechism "The Common Catechism")*.[[552]](#cite_note-Bent1994-563)[[553]](#cite_note-Phan2016-564)

The ecumenical, [monastic](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism") [Taizé Community](/wiki/Taiz%C3%A9_Community "Taizé Community") is notable for being composed of more than one hundred [brothers](/wiki/Monk "Monk") from Protestant and Catholic traditions.[[554]](#cite_note-taize-565) The community emphasizes the reconciliation of all denominations and its main church, located in [Taizé, Saône-et-Loire](/wiki/Taiz%C3%A9,_Sa%C3%B4ne-et-Loire "Taizé, Saône-et-Loire"), France, is named the "Church of Reconciliation".[[554]](#cite_note-taize-565) The community is internationally known, attracting over 100,000 young [pilgrims](/wiki/Christian_pilgrimage "Christian pilgrimage") annually.[[555]](#cite_note-566)

Steps towards reconciliation on a global level were taken in 1965 by the Catholic and Orthodox churches, mutually revoking the excommunications that marked their [Great Schism](/wiki/East-West_Schism "East-West Schism") in 1054;[[556]](#cite_note-567) the Anglican Catholic International Commission (ARCIC) working towards full communion between those churches since 1970;[[557]](#cite_note-568) and the [Lutheran World Federation](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation") and the Catholic Church signing the [Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification](/wiki/Joint_Declaration_on_the_Doctrine_of_Justification "Joint Declaration on the Doctrine of Justification") in 1999 to address conflicts at the root of the Protestant Reformation. In 2006, the [World Methodist Council](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"), representing all Methodist denominations, adopted the declaration.[[558]](#cite_note-569)

## Criticism, persecution, and apologetics

Main articles: [Criticism of Christianity](/wiki/Criticism_of_Christianity "Criticism of Christianity"), [Persecution of Christians](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians"), and [Christian apologetics](/wiki/Christian_apologetics "Christian apologetics")

See also: [Anti-Christian sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Christian_sentiment "Anti-Christian sentiment")

### Criticism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Bronze_statue_of_Giordano_Bruno_by_Ettore_Ferrari_%2C_Campo_de%27_Fiori%2C_Roma.jpg/250px-Bronze_statue_of_Giordano_Bruno_by_Ettore_Ferrari_%2C_Campo_de%27_Fiori%2C_Roma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronze_statue_of_Giordano_Bruno_by_Ettore_Ferrari_,_Campo_de%27_Fiori,_Roma.jpg)

The monument to [Giordano Bruno](/wiki/Giordano_Bruno "Giordano Bruno") in the place he was executed in Rome

Criticism of Christianity and Christians goes back to the [Apostolic Age](/wiki/Apostolic_Age "Apostolic Age"), with the New Testament recording friction between the followers of Jesus and the [Pharisees](/wiki/Pharisees "Pharisees") and [scribes](/wiki/Scribes "Scribes") (e.g., [Matthew 15:1–20](https://www.biblica.com/bible/?osis=niv:Matthew%2015:1–20) and [Mark 7:1–23](https://www.biblica.com/bible/?osis=niv:Mark%207:1–23)).[[559]](#cite_note-570) In the 2nd century, Christianity was criticized by the Jews on various grounds, e.g., that the prophecies of the Hebrew Bible could not have been fulfilled by Jesus, given that he did not have a successful life.[[560]](#cite_note-571) Additionally, a sacrifice to remove sins in advance, for everyone or as a human being, did not fit the Jewish sacrifice ritual; furthermore, [God in Judaism](/wiki/God_in_Judaism "God in Judaism") is said to judge people on their deeds instead of their beliefs.[[561]](#cite_note-572)[[562]](#cite_note-573) One of the first comprehensive attacks on Christianity came from the Greek philosopher [Celsus](/wiki/Celsus "Celsus"), who wrote *[The True Word](/wiki/The_True_Word "The True Word")*, a polemic criticizing Christians as being unprofitable members of society.[[563]](#cite_note-574)[[564]](#cite_note-575)[[565]](#cite_note-Olson1999-576) In response, the church father [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen") published his treatise *[Contra Celsum](/wiki/Contra_Celsum "Contra Celsum")*, or *Against Celsus*, a seminal work of Christian apologetics, which systematically addressed Celsus's criticisms and helped bring Christianity a level of academic respectability.[[566]](#cite_note-577)[[565]](#cite_note-Olson1999-576)

By the 3rd century, criticism of Christianity had mounted. Wild rumors about Christians were widely circulated, claiming that they were [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") and that, as part of their rituals, they devoured human infants and engaged in incestuous orgies.[[567]](#cite_note-578)[[568]](#cite_note-579) The [Neoplatonist](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism") philosopher [Porphyry](/wiki/Porphyry_(philosopher) "Porphyry (philosopher)") wrote the fifteen-volume *Adversus Christianos* as a comprehensive attack on Christianity, in part building on the teachings of [Plotinus](/wiki/Plotinus "Plotinus").[[569]](#cite_note-580)[[570]](#cite_note-581)

By the 12th century, the [Mishneh Torah](/wiki/Mishneh_Torah "Mishneh Torah") (i.e., [Rabbi](/wiki/Rabbi "Rabbi") [Moses Maimonides](/wiki/Moses_Maimonides "Moses Maimonides")) was criticizing Christianity on the grounds of idol worship, in that Christians attributed divinity to Jesus, who had a physical body.[[571]](#cite_note-582) In the 19th century, [Nietzsche](/wiki/Nietzsche "Nietzsche") began to write a series of polemics on the "unnatural" teachings of Christianity (e.g. sexual abstinence), and continued his criticism of Christianity to the end of his life.[[572]](#cite_note-583) In the 20th century, the philosopher [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") expressed his criticism of Christianity in *[Why I Am Not a Christian](/wiki/Why_I_Am_Not_a_Christian "Why I Am Not a Christian")*, formulating his rejection of Christianity.[[573]](#cite_note-584)

Criticism of Christianity continues to date, e.g. [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") and [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") theologians criticize the doctrine of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") held by most Christians, stating that this doctrine in effect assumes that there are three gods, running against the basic tenet of [monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism").[[574]](#cite_note-585) New Testament scholar [Robert M. Price](/wiki/Robert_M._Price "Robert M. Price") has outlined the possibility that some Bible stories are based partly on myth in *The Christ Myth Theory and its problems*.[[575]](#cite_note-586)

### Persecution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Kharput_Christian_refugees_-_C.D.Morris_-_National_Geographic%2C_Nov._1925.jpg/250px-Kharput_Christian_refugees_-_C.D.Morris_-_National_Geographic%2C_Nov._1925.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kharput_Christian_refugees_-_C.D.Morris_-_National_Geographic,_Nov._1925.jpg)

Christians fleeing their homes in the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), c. 1922. Many Christians were persecuted and killed during the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), [Greek genocide](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide"), and [Assyrian genocide](/wiki/Assyrian_genocide "Assyrian genocide").[[576]](#cite_note-587)

Christians are one of the most [persecuted](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians "Persecution of Christians") religious groups in the world, especially in the [Middle-East](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Middle_East "Christianity in the Middle East"), [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") and South and East Asia.[[577]](#cite_note-588)[[578]](#cite_note-589)[[579]](#cite_note-Wintour_2019_Guardian-590) In 2017, [Open Doors](/wiki/Open_Doors "Open Doors") estimated approximately 260 million Christians are subjected annually to "high, very high, or extreme persecution"[[580]](#cite_note-591) with North Korea considered the most hazardous nation for Christians.[[581]](#cite_note-592)[[582]](#cite_note-593) In 2019, a report[[583]](#cite_note-594)[[584]](#cite_note-Mounstephen_2019_FCO_final_rep-595) commissioned by the United Kingdom's [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Affairs "Secretary of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs") of the [Foreign and Commonwealth Office](/wiki/Foreign_and_Commonwealth_Office "Foreign and Commonwealth Office") (FCO) to investigate global persecution of Christians found persecution has increased, and is highest in the Middle East, North Africa, India, China, North Korea, and Latin America, among others, and that it is global and not limited to Islamic states.[[585]](#cite_note-596)[[584]](#cite_note-Mounstephen_2019_FCO_final_rep-595) This investigation found that approximately 80% of persecuted believers worldwide are Christians.[[579]](#cite_note-Wintour_2019_Guardian-590)

### Apologetics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/SummaTheologiae.jpg/250px-SummaTheologiae.jpg)](/wiki/File:SummaTheologiae.jpg)

A copy of the *[Summa Theologica](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")* by [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas")

Christian apologetics aims to present a [rational](/wiki/Reason "Reason") basis for Christianity. The word "apologetic" (Greek: ἀπολογητικός *apologētikos*) comes from the Greek verb ἀπολογέομαι *apologeomai*, meaning "(I) speak in defense of".[[586]](#cite_note-597) Christian apologetics has taken many forms over the centuries, starting with Paul the Apostle. The philosopher [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") presented five arguments for God's existence in the *[Summa Theologica](/wiki/Summa_Theologica "Summa Theologica")*, while his *[Summa contra Gentiles](/wiki/Summa_contra_Gentiles "Summa contra Gentiles")* was a major apologetic work.[[587]](#cite_note-598)[[588]](#cite_note-599) Another famous apologist, [G. K. Chesterton](/wiki/G._K._Chesterton "G. K. Chesterton"), wrote in the early twentieth century about the benefits of religion and, specifically, Christianity. Famous for his use of paradox, Chesterton explained that while Christianity had the most mysteries, it was the most practical religion.[[589]](#cite_note-600)[[590]](#cite_note-601) He pointed to the [advance of Christian civilizations](/wiki/Role_of_the_Christian_Church_in_civilization "Role of the Christian Church in civilization") as proof of its practicality.[[591]](#cite_note-602) The physicist and priest [John Polkinghorne](/wiki/John_Polkinghorne "John Polkinghorne"), in his *[Questions of Truth](/wiki/Questions_of_Truth "Questions of Truth")*, discusses the subject of [religion and science](/wiki/Religion_and_science "Religion and science"), a topic that other Christian apologists such as [Ravi Zacharias](/wiki/Ravi_Zacharias "Ravi Zacharias"), [John Lennox](/wiki/John_Lennox "John Lennox"), and [William Lane Craig](/wiki/William_Lane_Craig "William Lane Craig") have engaged, with the latter two men opining that the [inflationary Big Bang model](/wiki/Religious_interpretations_of_the_Big_Bang_theory "Religious interpretations of the Big Bang theory") is evidence for the [existence of God](/wiki/Existence_of_God "Existence of God").[[592]](#cite_note-603) [Creationist apologetics](/wiki/Creationist_apologetics "Creationist apologetics") is apologetics that aims to defend [creationism](/wiki/Creationism "Creationism").

Some [English translations of the New Testament](/wiki/English_translations_of_the_Bible "English translations of the Bible") capitalize 'the Way' (e.g. the [New King James Version](/wiki/New_King_James_Version "New King James Version") and the [English Standard Version](/wiki/English_Standard_Version "English Standard Version")), indicating that this was how 'the new religion seemed then to be designated'[[13]](#cite_note-17) whereas others treat the phrase as indicative—'the way',[[14]](#cite_note-18) 'that way'[[15]](#cite_note-19) or 'the way of the Lord'.[[16]](#cite_note-20) The [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") version reads, "the way of God" and the [Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate") Latin version, "the way of the Lord".[[17]](#cite_note-21)

"Hippolytus in the *Apostolic Tradition* directed that Christians should pray seven times a day – on rising, at the lighting of the evening lamp, at bedtime, at midnight, and also, if at home, at the third, sixth and ninth hours of the day, being hours associated with Christ's Passion. "Not only the content of early Christian prayer was rooted in Jewish tradition; its daily structure too initially followed a Jewish pattern, with prayer times in the early morning, at noon and in the evening. Later (in the course of the second century), this pattern combined with another one; namely prayer times in the evening, at midnight and in the morning. As a result seven 'hours of prayer' emerged, which later became the monastic 'hours' and are still treated as 'standard' prayer times in many churches today. They are roughly equivalent to midnight, 6 a.m., 9 a.m., noon, 3 p.m., 6 p.m. and 9 p.m. Prayer positions included prostration, kneeling and standing. ... Crosses made of wood or stone, or painted on walls or laid out as mosaics, were also in use, at first not directly as objections of veneration but in order to 'orientate' the direction of prayer (i.e. towards the east, Latin *oriens*)."
